narrative:
"And thus, Glaucon, after the argument has gone a weary way, the true and the false philosophers have at length appeared in view."

"I do not think," he said, "that the way could have been shortened."

"I suppose not," I said; "and yet I believe that we might have had a better view of both of them if the discussion could have been confined to this one subject and if there were not many other questions awaiting us, which he who desires to see in what respect the life of the just differs from that of the unjust must consider."

"And what is the next question?" he asked.

"Surely," I said, "the one which follows next in order. Inasmuch as philosophers only are able to grasp the eternal and unchangeable, and those who wander in the region of the many and variable are not philosophers, I must ask you which of the two classes should be the rulers of our State?"

"And how can we rightly answer that question?"

"Whichever of the two are best able to guard the laws and institutions of our State -- let them be our guardians."

"Very good."

"Neither," I said, "can there be any question that the guardian who is to keep anything should have eyes rather than no eyes?"
refashioned:
"So, Glaucon, after this long and winding road, we've finally gotten a clear look at both the genuine and the counterfeit philosophers."

"I don't think there was any shortcut," he said.

"Probably not," I replied, "though we might have seen them more clearly if we could have focused on this one topic alone. But there are still many questions waiting for us -- questions that anyone who wants to understand how the just life differs from the unjust life must eventually face."

"So what's the next question?" he asked.

"The natural follow-up," I said. "Since only philosophers can grasp what is eternal and unchanging, while those who drift among constantly shifting particulars are not philosophers -- which of these two groups should rule our state?"

"How do we decide that?"

"Whichever group is best equipped to safeguard the laws and institutions of our state -- they should be our guardians."

"That makes sense."

"And surely," I said, "no one would argue that a guardian meant to protect something should be blind rather than sighted?"
elegant: After a long journey through argument, the true philosophers are finally distinguished from the false. The next question follows naturally: since only philosophers grasp the eternal and unchanging, while others wander among shifting particulars, philosophers should be the rulers. The guardians of a state must have vision, not blindness.
summary: Socrates and Glaucon conclude their distinction between true and false philosophers. Socrates argues that since only philosophers grasp eternal truths, they are the natural candidates to rule as guardians of the state.
distilled: Only those who can perceive eternal truths should govern, for a guardian must have sight rather than blindness.
notes: Speakers: Socrates and Glaucon -- Continues from Book V's discussion of true vs. false philosophers -- 'The eternal and unchangeable' refers to the Forms (Platonic metaphysics) -- 'The many and variable' refers to the world of appearances/becoming -- Guardians as rulers established in Books II-III -- The sight/blindness metaphor anticipates the Sun analogy and Cave allegory (Book VII)

narrative: "And are not those who are verily and indeed wanting in the knowledge of the true being of each thing, and who have in their souls no clear pattern, and are unable as with a painter's eye to look at the absolute truth and to that original to repair, and having perfect vision of the other world to order the laws about beauty, goodness, justice in this, if not already ordered, and to guard and preserve the order of them -- are not such persons, I ask, simply blind?"
refashioned: "And what about those who truly lack knowledge of what each thing really is -- who have no clear model in their minds, who cannot look toward absolute truth as a painter looks at a subject, and who cannot use that perfect vision of the higher reality to establish and maintain laws about beauty, goodness, and justice in this world? Aren't such people essentially blind?"
elegant: Those who lack knowledge of true being, who carry no clear pattern of reality in their souls, and who cannot look to absolute truth as a model for ordering laws about beauty, goodness, and justice -- such people are simply blind.
summary: Socrates argues that anyone without knowledge of the true nature of things -- without a clear model of absolute truth to guide their lawmaking about beauty, goodness, and justice -- is effectively blind.
distilled: Without knowledge of absolute truth as a guiding pattern, those who would order laws about beauty, goodness, and justice are simply blind.
notes: Speaker: Socrates (rhetorical question to Glaucon) -- The 'painter's eye' metaphor: the philosopher-ruler must have a model (paradeigma) of the Forms to copy from -- 'True being of each thing' = the Form or Idea of each thing -- Anticipates the philosopher-as-artist metaphor developed later in Book VI (6.47-48) -- The blindness metaphor connects to the Cave allegory in Book VII

narrative:
"Truly," he replied, "they are much in that condition."

"And shall they be our guardians when there are others who, besides being their equals in experience and falling short of them in no particular of virtue, also know the very truth of each thing?"

"There can be no reason," he said, "for rejecting those who have this greatest of all great qualities; they must always have the first place unless they fail in some other respect."

"Suppose then," I said, "that we determine how far they can unite this and the other excellences."

"By all means."

"In the first place, as we began by observing, the nature of the philosopher has to be ascertained. We must come to an understanding about him, and, when we have done so, then, if I am not mistaken, we shall also acknowledge that such an union of qualities is possible, and that those in whom they are united, and those only, should be rulers in the State."

"What do you mean?"

"Let us suppose that philosophical minds always love knowledge of a sort which shows them the eternal nature not varying from generation and corruption."

"Agreed."

"And further," I said, "let us agree that they are lovers of all true being; there is no part whether greater or less, or more or less honourable, which they are willing to renounce; as we said before of the lover and the man of ambition."

"True."

"And if they are to be what we were describing, is there not another quality which they should also possess?"

"What quality?"

"Truthfulness: they will never intentionally receive into their mind falsehood, which is their detestation, and they will love the truth."

"Yes, that may be safely affirmed of them."

"'May be,' my friend," I replied, "is not the word; say rather 'must be affirmed:' for he whose nature is amorous of anything cannot help loving all that belongs or is akin to the object of his affections."

"Right," he said.

"And is there anything more akin to wisdom than truth?"

"How can there be?"

"Can the same nature be a lover of wisdom and a lover of falsehood?"

"Never."
refashioned:
"Indeed," he replied, "they really are in that condition."

"Then should they be our guardians when there are others who match them in experience, equal them in every virtue, and also possess knowledge of the true nature of each thing?"

"There's no reason," he said, "to reject those who have this greatest of all qualities. They should always hold first place, unless they fall short in some other way."

"Then let's work out how far they can combine this quality with the other excellences," I said.

"Absolutely."

"First, we need to define the philosopher's nature. Once we've done that, I believe we'll see that such a combination of qualities is indeed possible -- and that only those who possess them all should rule."

"What do you mean?"

"Let's start with this: philosophical minds are drawn to knowledge that reveals the eternal -- what doesn't shift with generation and decay."

"Agreed."

"And they are lovers of all true being -- they won't renounce any part of it, whether great or small, honored or humble. Like the true lover or the truly ambitious person, they want the whole of what they desire."

"True."

"And there's another quality they must have. What is it? Truthfulness. They will never willingly admit falsehood into their minds. They detest it and love truth."

"That can safely be said of them."

"Not just 'safely said,' my friend -- it must be said. Anyone whose nature is passionate about something inevitably loves everything connected to it."

"Right."

"And what is more closely connected to wisdom than truth?"

"Nothing."

"Can the same person be a lover of wisdom and a lover of falsehood?"

"Never."
elegant: Those who know the truth of each thing and also possess every other virtue should be the guardians -- no one else has a stronger claim. The philosophical nature must be defined: philosophers love knowledge of the eternal, embrace all true being without exception, and are devoted to truth. Since wisdom and truth are intimately connected, a lover of wisdom cannot also be a lover of falsehood.
summary: Socrates argues that philosopher-guardians must combine knowledge of eternal truth with all other virtues. He begins defining the philosopher's nature: they love knowledge of the unchanging, embrace all being, and are inherently truthful, since wisdom and truth are inseparable.
distilled: The philosopher's nature requires love of eternal knowledge, embrace of all being, and devotion to truth -- for wisdom and falsehood are incompatible.
notes: Speakers: Socrates and Glaucon -- 'Eternal nature not varying from generation and corruption' = the Forms, as opposed to the sensible world of becoming -- The analogy to the lover: as an erotic lover desires everything about the beloved, so the philosopher desires all truth (cf. Book V, 474c-475c) -- Truthfulness as a necessary trait of the philosopher anticipates the corruption argument later in Book VI -- This passage begins the systematic enumeration of the philosopher's virtues

narrative:
"The true lover of learning then must from his earliest youth, as far as in him lies, desire all truth?"

"Assuredly."

"But then again, as we know by experience, he whose desires are strong in one direction will have them weaker in others; they will be like a stream which has been drawn off into another channel."

"True."

"He whose desires are drawn towards knowledge in every form will be absorbed in the pleasures of the soul, and will hardly feel bodily pleasure -- I mean, if he be a true philosopher and not a sham one."

"That is most certain."

"Such an one is sure to be temperate and the reverse of covetous; for the motives which make another man desirous of having and spending, have no place in his character."

"Very true."

"Another criterion of the philosophical nature has also to be considered."

"What is that?"

"There should be no secret corner of illiberality; nothing can be more antagonistic than meanness to a soul which is ever longing after the whole of things both divine and human."

"Most true," he replied.

"Then how can he who has magnificence of mind and is the spectator of all time and all existence, think much of human life?"

"He cannot."

"Or can such an one account death fearful?"

"No indeed."

"Then the cowardly and mean nature has no part in true philosophy?"

"Certainly not."

"Or again: can he who is harmoniously constituted, who is not covetous or mean, or a boaster, or a coward -- can he, I say, ever be unjust or hard in his dealings?"

"Impossible."

"Then you will soon observe whether a man is just and gentle, or rude and unsociable; these are the signs which distinguish even in youth the philosophical nature from the unphilosophical."

"True."

"There is another point which should be remarked."

"What point?"

"Whether he has or has not a pleasure in learning; for no one will love that which gives him pain, and in which after much toil he makes little progress."

"Certainly not."

"And again, if he is forgetful and retains nothing of what he learns, will he not be an empty vessel?"
refashioned:
"So the true lover of learning must, from earliest youth, desire all truth?"

"Of course."

"But we know from experience that when someone's desires flow strongly in one direction, they flow more weakly in others -- like a stream diverted into a new channel."

"True."

"Someone whose desires are drawn entirely toward knowledge will be absorbed in the pleasures of the mind and will barely notice bodily pleasures -- if he's a genuine philosopher, not a pretender."

"Absolutely."

"Such a person will naturally be temperate and free from greed. The drives that make others obsess over acquiring and spending simply don't exist in his character."

"Very true."

"There's another criterion we need to examine."

"What's that?"

"There must be no hidden corner of small-mindedness. Nothing is more opposed to a soul that constantly reaches toward the whole of things, both divine and human, than pettiness."

"Absolutely," he replied.

"And how could someone with greatness of mind, who contemplates all of time and all of existence, place much importance on human life?"

"He couldn't."

"Could such a person consider death something to fear?"

"Not at all."

"So cowardice and small-mindedness have no place in genuine philosophy?"

"Certainly not."

"And can someone who is harmoniously composed -- neither greedy, nor petty, nor boastful, nor cowardly -- ever be unjust or cruel?"

"Impossible."

"So you can tell early on whether someone is just and gentle or rough and antisocial. These are the markers that distinguish the philosophical nature from the unphilosophical, even in youth."

"True."

"One more thing to consider."

"What?"

"Whether he takes pleasure in learning. No one will love what causes him pain and where much effort yields little progress."

"Of course not."

"And if he's forgetful and retains nothing of what he learns -- won't he be an empty vessel?"
elegant: The true philosopher's desires flow toward knowledge, leaving little current for bodily pleasures -- making him naturally temperate and free from greed. He must be free of pettiness, for a soul reaching toward the whole of existence cannot be small-minded. Such greatness of mind makes him fearless before death and incapable of injustice. He must also take pleasure in learning and have a strong memory, or his efforts will be fruitless.
summary: Socrates continues cataloguing the philosopher's traits: desires channeled toward knowledge produce natural temperance; greatness of mind yields courage before death and freedom from injustice; and genuine philosophers must take pleasure in learning and possess strong memories.
distilled: The philosopher's desires, flowing entirely toward knowledge, naturally produce temperance, magnanimity, courage, justice, and a strong memory.
notes: Speakers: Socrates and Glaucon -- The 'stream' metaphor for desire: psychic energy directed toward knowledge leaves less for bodily appetites -- 'Spectator of all time and all existence' (theoros) -- a famous phrase describing philosophical contemplation -- The enumeration of virtues (temperance, courage, justice, magnanimity) parallels the cardinal virtues discussed in Book IV -- 'Empty vessel' = a mind that cannot retain knowledge is useless for philosophy -- These traits will later be shown to make philosophers vulnerable to corruption (6.17)

narrative:
"That is certain. Labouring in vain, he must end in hating himself and his fruitless occupation?"

"Yes."

"Then a soul which forgets cannot be ranked among genuine philosophic natures; we must insist that the philosopher should have a good memory?"

"Certainly."

"And once more, the inharmonious and unseemly nature can only tend to disproportion?"

"Undoubtedly."

"And do you consider truth to be akin to proportion or to disproportion?"

"To proportion."

"Then, besides other qualities, we must try to find a naturally well-proportioned and gracious mind, which will move spontaneously towards the true being of everything."

"Certainly."

"Well, and do not all these qualities, which we have been enumerating, go together, and are they not, in a manner, necessary to a soul, which is to have a full and perfect participation of being?"

"They are absolutely necessary," he replied.

"And must not that be a blameless study which he only can pursue who has the gift of a good memory, and is quick to learn -- noble, gracious, the friend of truth, justice, courage, temperance, who are his kindred?"

"The god of jealousy himself," he said, "could find no fault with such a study."

"And to men like him," I said, "when perfected by years and education, and to these only you will entrust the State."
refashioned:
"Exactly. Laboring in vain, he'd end up hating both himself and his fruitless work."

"Yes."

"So a forgetful soul doesn't belong among genuine philosophers. We must insist the philosopher have a good memory."

"Certainly."

"And a disharmonious, graceless nature can only tend toward disproportion."

"Without a doubt."

"And is truth more akin to proportion or to disproportion?"

"To proportion."

"Then we must also look for a naturally well-proportioned and graceful mind -- one that moves spontaneously toward the true nature of everything."

"Of course."

"Now, don't all these qualities we've been listing go together? Aren't they all necessary for a soul that is to fully participate in being?"

"They are absolutely necessary," he replied.

"And isn't it a blameless pursuit -- one that can only be followed by someone with a good memory, quick to learn, noble, graceful, a friend of truth, justice, courage, and temperance?"

"Even the god of jealousy," he said, "couldn't find fault with such a pursuit."

"And to people like this," I said, "when they've been perfected by years and education -- to these alone you will entrust the state."
elegant: A forgetful soul is no philosopher. Truth is akin to proportion, so the philosophical mind must be naturally harmonious and graceful, drawn spontaneously toward true being. All these qualities -- memory, quick learning, nobility, grace, and friendship with truth, justice, courage, and temperance -- are necessary for full participation in being. Only those perfected by years and education should be entrusted with the state.
summary: Socrates concludes the portrait of the philosophical nature: good memory, natural proportion, and graceful intelligence are essential. All these virtues form an inseparable whole, and only those who possess them all, refined through education, should be entrusted with governance.
distilled: The complete philosophical nature -- harmonious, well-memoried, graceful, and allied with every virtue -- is the only nature fit to be entrusted with the state.
notes: Speakers: Socrates and Glaucon -- 'The god of jealousy' (Momus) = Greek god of blame and criticism -- even he could find no fault -- Truth as akin to proportion (symmetria) reflects Pythagorean and Platonic aesthetics -- 'Full and perfect participation of being' = methexis, the soul's relation to the Forms -- This concludes Chapter 6.1's portrait of the ideal philosopher -- The list of virtues (memory, quick learning, nobility, truth, justice, courage, temperance) will be ironically revisited when Socrates explains how these same qualities lead to corruption (6.17)

narrative: Here Adeimantus interposed and said: "To these statements, Socrates, no one can offer a reply; but when you talk in this way, a strange feeling passes over the minds of your hearers: They fancy that they are led astray a little at each step in the argument, owing to their own want of skill in asking and answering questions; these littles accumulate, and at the end of the discussion they are found to have sustained a mighty overthrow and all their former notions appear to be turned upside down. And as unskilful players of draughts are at last shut up by their more skilful adversaries and have no piece to move, so they too find themselves shut up at last; for they have nothing to say in this new game of which words are the counters; and yet all the time they are in the right. The observation is suggested to me by what is now occurring. For any one of us might say, that although in words he is not able to meet you at each step of the argument, he sees as a fact that the votaries of philosophy, when they carry on the study, not only in youth as a part of education, but as the pursuit of their maturer years, most of them become strange monsters, not to say utter rogues, and that those who may be considered the best of them are made useless to the world by the very study which you extol."
refashioned:
At this point Adeimantus broke in: "No one can argue with what you're saying, Socrates, but your listeners get a strange feeling when you talk like this. They sense they're being led slightly off course at each step -- not through any trick of yours, but because they lack the skill to challenge you properly. These small concessions pile up, and by the end they find their entire position has been overturned. It's like being beaten at checkers by a more skilled player -- eventually you have no moves left. They're checkmated in this game of words, and yet they feel they were right all along.

"This is exactly what's happening now. Any of us could point out that, whatever the logic says, the facts tell a different story: people who pursue philosophy not just in youth but as a lifelong vocation mostly turn out to be cranks, if not outright scoundrels, and even the best of them are made useless to society by the very study you're praising."
elegant: Adeimantus objects: no one can refute Socrates in argument, but listeners feel led astray step by step, like unskilled players checkmated in a word-game. Yet the facts remain stubborn -- most lifelong philosophers become eccentric or corrupt, and even the best are rendered useless by the very study Socrates champions.
summary: Adeimantus interrupts to voice the common objection: while no one can defeat Socrates in argument, observable reality shows that most devoted philosophers become either strange or corrupt, and even the best are made useless to society.
distilled: However compelling the argument for philosophy, the facts show that most philosophers become either corrupt or useless.
notes: Speakers: Adeimantus interrupts Socrates -- Adeimantus is Plato's brother and frequent interlocutor -- The 'draughts' (pessoi/checkers) metaphor illustrates how dialectical skill can win arguments even against correct intuitions -- This objection is crucial: it forces Socrates to explain why philosophy gets a bad reputation -- The distinction between 'useless' philosophers and 'corrupt' ones structures the rest of Book VI -- Echoes the charge against Socrates himself at his trial (Apology)

narrative:
"Well, and do you think that those who say so are wrong?"

"I cannot tell," he replied; "but I should like to know what is your opinion."

"Hear my answer; I am of opinion that they are quite right."

"Then how can you be justified in saying that cities will not cease from evil until philosophers rule in them, when philosophers are acknowledged by us to be of no use to them?"

"You ask a question," I said, "to which a reply can only be given in a parable."

"Yes, Socrates; and that is a way of speaking to which you are not at all accustomed, I suppose."
refashioned:
"And do you think the people who say this are wrong?"

"I'm not sure," he replied. "But I'd like to hear your view."

"Here's my answer: I think they're entirely right."

"Then how can you justify claiming that cities won't be free of evil until philosophers rule them -- when you've just agreed that philosophers are useless to cities?"

"That question," I said, "can only be answered through a parable."

"A parable, Socrates? That's certainly not your usual style," he said with a hint of irony.
elegant: Socrates agrees that philosophers are indeed seen as useless -- then faces the paradox: how can philosopher-kings save cities if philosophers are useless to them? He says this can only be answered through a parable.
summary: Socrates surprisingly agrees that philosophers are viewed as useless. When Adeimantus asks how he can then advocate philosopher-kings, Socrates says the answer requires a parable.
distilled: Socrates concedes philosophers appear useless, but says a parable is needed to explain why the fault lies not with philosophy but with society.
notes: Speakers: Socrates and Adeimantus -- Socrates' agreement that philosophers are useless is a dialectical move -- he will shift the blame from philosophy to the state -- The 'parable' introduces the famous Ship of State allegory -- Adeimantus' sarcasm ('not at all accustomed') is ironic, since Socrates frequently uses analogies and myths -- This sets up one of the Republic's most famous extended metaphors

narrative:
"I perceive," I said, "that you are vastly amused at having plunged me into such a hopeless discussion; but now hear the parable, and then you will be still more amused at the meagreness of my imagination: for the manner in which the best men are treated in their own States is so grievous that no single thing on earth is comparable to it; and therefore, if I am to plead their cause, I must have recourse to fiction, and put together a figure made up of many things, like the fabulous unions of goats and stags which are found in pictures.

Imagine then a fleet or a ship in which there is a captain who is taller and stronger than any of the crew, but he is a little deaf and has a similar infirmity in sight, and his knowledge of navigation is not much better. The sailors are quarrelling with one another about the steering -- every one is of opinion that he has a right to steer, though he has never learned the art of navigation and cannot tell who taught him or when he learned, and will further assert that it cannot be taught, and they are ready to cut in pieces any one who says the contrary. They throng about the captain, begging and praying him to commit the helm to them; and if at any time they do not prevail, but others are preferred to them, they kill the others or throw them overboard, and having first chained up the noble captain's senses with drink or some narcotic drug, they mutiny and take possession of the ship and make free with the stores; thus, eating and drinking, they proceed on their voyage in such a manner as might be expected of them.

Him who is their partisan and cleverly aids them in their plot for getting the ship out of the captain's hands into their own whether by force or persuasion, they compliment with the name of sailor, pilot, able seaman, and abuse the other sort of man, whom they call a good-for-nothing; but that the true pilot must pay attention to the year and seasons and sky and stars and winds, and whatever else belongs to his art, if he intends to be really qualified for the command of a ship, and that he must and will be the steerer, whether other people like or not -- the possibility of this union of authority with the steerer's art has never seriously entered into their thoughts or been made part of their calling."
refashioned:
"I can see you're quite amused at having cornered me," I said, "but hear the parable, and you'll be even more amused by my limited imagination. The way the best people are treated in their own states is so terrible that nothing else compares, so I have to resort to a composite image -- like those mythical paintings of goat-stag hybrids.

Picture a ship whose captain is bigger and stronger than anyone in the crew, but somewhat deaf, somewhat near-sighted, and not much of a navigator. The sailors are all fighting over who should steer, each one convinced he has the right to the helm -- despite never having learned navigation, being unable to name any teacher or time of study, and insisting that navigation can't even be taught. They're ready to tear apart anyone who disagrees.

They crowd around the captain, begging and pleading for control of the helm. When they don't get their way, they kill or throw overboard whoever does. They drug the captain into a stupor, seize the ship, raid the stores, and sail on in a drunken party -- exactly as you'd expect.

Anyone who helps them scheme to take control -- whether through force or manipulation -- they honor with titles like 'skilled sailor,' 'true navigator,' 'master seaman.' Meanwhile, they mock the person who actually understands navigation as a 'useless stargazer.' The idea that a real pilot must study the seasons, the sky, the stars, and the winds -- that genuine navigational knowledge and authority over the ship must go together -- this has never seriously crossed their minds."
elegant: Socrates offers the Ship of State parable: a ship's captain is strong but impaired in sight, hearing, and navigational knowledge. The crew fights over the helm despite knowing nothing of navigation, drugs the captain, seizes control, and rewards schemers as 'true sailors' while mocking anyone who studies the stars and winds as useless. The notion that genuine navigational knowledge should confer authority has never occurred to them.
summary: Socrates introduces the Ship of State allegory: an impaired captain, ignorant mutinous sailors who seize control, and a true navigator dismissed as a useless stargazer -- illustrating why genuine philosophers are scorned in democratic societies.
distilled: In the Ship of State, ignorant sailors seize the helm and mock the true navigator as useless -- just as democracies dismiss genuine philosophers.
notes: Speakers: Socrates (extended parable, with Adeimantus listening) -- The Ship of State: one of the Republic's most famous allegories -- The captain = the demos/people (strong but impaired in judgment) -- The quarreling sailors = democratic politicians competing for power -- The true pilot = the philosopher who studies reality -- 'Good-for-nothing' and 'star-gazer' = common insults against philosophers (cf. Aristophanes' Clouds mocking Socrates) -- The 'fabulous unions of goats and stags' = chimeric composite images -- This parable directly addresses why philosophers appear useless in democratic states

narrative:
"Now in vessels which are in a state of mutiny and by sailors who are mutineers, how will the true pilot be regarded? Will he not be called by them a prater, a star-gazer, a good-for-nothing?"

"Of course," said Adeimantus.

"Then you will hardly need," I said, "to hear the interpretation of the figure, which describes the true philosopher in his relation to the State; for you understand already."

"Certainly."

"Then suppose you now take this parable to the gentleman who is surprised at finding that philosophers have no honour in their cities; explain it to him and try to convince him that their having honour would be far more extraordinary."
refashioned:
"Now, on a ship in the grip of mutiny, how would the true navigator be regarded by the mutinous sailors? Wouldn't they call him a babbler, a stargazer, a waste of space?"

"Of course," said Adeimantus.

"Then I hardly need to spell out what this parable means for the philosopher's relationship to the state -- you already understand."

"I do."

"So take this parable to anyone who's surprised that philosophers receive no honor in their cities. Try to convince him that it would be far more surprising if they did."
elegant: On a mutinous ship, the true navigator will inevitably be called a useless stargazer. The parable's meaning is clear: in a state run by the ignorant, it would be far more extraordinary if philosophers were honored than if they were not.
summary: Socrates draws the lesson from the Ship of State: in a mutinous political order, the true philosopher will naturally be dismissed. The real surprise would be if philosophers were honored in such cities.
distilled: On a mutinous ship, the true navigator is scorned -- and in a disordered state, it would be astonishing if philosophers were honored.
notes: Speakers: Socrates and Adeimantus -- This is the interpretive key to the Ship of State allegory -- 'Prater' and 'star-gazer' = terms of abuse for intellectuals in Athenian democracy -- Socrates reverses the expectation: dishonor is the norm, honor would be the anomaly -- The argument shifts blame from philosophers to the political order -- Cf. Socrates' own treatment by Athens, culminating in his trial and execution (Apology, Crito, Phaedo)

narrative: "I will. Say to him, that, in deeming the best votaries of philosophy to be useless to the rest of the world, he is right; but also tell him to attribute their uselessness to the fault of those who will not use them, and not to themselves. The pilot should not humbly beg the sailors to be commanded by him -- that is not the order of nature; neither are 'the wise to go to the doors of the rich' -- the ingenious author of this saying told a lie -- but the truth is, that, when a man is ill, whether he be rich or poor, to the physician he must go, and he who wants to be governed, to him who is able to govern. The ruler who is good for anything ought not to beg his subjects to be ruled by him; although the present governors of mankind are of a different stamp; they may be justly compared to the mutinous sailors, and the true helmsmen to those who are called by them good-for-nothings and star-gazers."
refashioned:
"Tell him this: he's right that the best philosophers appear useless to society. But tell him to blame those who refuse to use them, not the philosophers themselves.

A pilot shouldn't have to beg the sailors to accept his command -- that's not how nature works. And the old saying that 'the wise should go knocking on the doors of the rich' is a lie. The truth is the opposite: when you're sick, whether rich or poor, you go to the doctor. And when you need governance, you go to the one who knows how to govern.

A ruler who is truly capable shouldn't have to beg people to accept his rule. Yet today's rulers are exactly like those mutinous sailors, and the true helmsmen are the ones they dismiss as useless stargazers."
elegant: The best philosophers are indeed useless -- but the fault lies with those who refuse to use them. A pilot doesn't beg sailors to obey; a patient goes to the doctor, not the reverse. The competent ruler shouldn't beg to rule. Today's rulers are the mutinous sailors; the true helmsmen are those they dismiss as stargazers.
summary: Socrates argues that philosophers' uselessness is society's fault, not philosophy's. Just as patients must go to doctors and not vice versa, those who need governance must seek out those who can govern. Current rulers resemble mutinous sailors, while true philosophers are the dismissed helmsmen.
distilled: Philosophers appear useless because society refuses to use them -- the sick must go to the doctor, not the doctor to the sick.
notes: Speakers: Socrates (continuing to Adeimantus) -- 'The wise to go to the doors of the rich' = a proverb attributed to Simonides, which Socrates rejects -- The doctor analogy: governance requires expertise, just as medicine does (a recurring Platonic argument, cf. Gorgias) -- This inverts the democratic assumption that rulers should court the people -- The philosopher should not campaign for power; those who need governance should seek the philosopher -- Anticipates the 'reluctant ruler' theme: the best rulers are those who don't want to rule (cf. Book VII, 520d-521b)

narrative:
"Precisely so," he said.

"For these reasons, and among men like these, philosophy, the noblest pursuit of all, is not likely to be much esteemed by those of the opposite faction; not that the greatest and most lasting injury is done to her by her opponents, but by her own professing followers, the same of whom you suppose the accuser to say, that the greater number of them are arrant rogues, and the best are useless; in which opinion I agreed."
refashioned:
"Exactly," he said.

"So for these reasons, and among people like these, philosophy -- the noblest pursuit there is -- won't be valued by its opponents. But here's the crucial point: the greatest and most lasting damage to philosophy isn't done by its enemies. It's done by its own self-proclaimed followers -- the very people the accuser describes as mostly rogues, with even the best being useless. And as I said, I agree with that assessment."
elegant: Philosophy, the noblest pursuit, is damaged most not by its enemies but by its own professing followers -- most of whom are rogues, and even the best of whom appear useless.
summary: Socrates makes a key distinction: philosophy's greatest injury comes not from its opponents but from those who falsely claim to practice it, most of whom are corrupt and even the best of whom seem useless.
distilled: Philosophy is harmed most not by its enemies but by its own unworthy practitioners.
notes: Speakers: Socrates and Adeimantus -- Philosophy personified as a noble figure being dishonored -- The distinction between external opponents and internal pretenders is central to Book VI -- This sets up the later discussion of how philosophical natures are corrupted (6.17ff) and how unworthy people enter philosophy (6.31-32) -- The 'arrant rogues' = those who use philosophical training for sophistry or personal gain

narrative:
"Yes. And the reason why the good are useless has now been explained?"

"True."

"Then shall we proceed to show that the corruption of the majority is also unavoidable, and that this is not to be laid to the charge of philosophy any more than the other?"

"By all means."

"And let us ask and answer in turn, first going back to the description of the gentle and noble nature. Truth, as you will remember, was his leader, whom he followed always and in all things; failing in this, he was an impostor, and had no part or lot in true philosophy."

"Yes, that was said."

"Well, and is not this one quality, to mention no others, greatly at variance with present notions of him?"
refashioned:
"Yes. So we've now explained why the good philosophers appear useless?"

"We have."

"Then shall we move on to show that the corruption of the majority is also unavoidable -- and that this too cannot be blamed on philosophy itself?"

"By all means."

"Let's return to our description of the gentle and noble nature. You'll recall that truth was his guide, whom he followed in everything. Without that, he was a fraud with no claim to genuine philosophy."

"Yes, that's what we said."

"And isn't even this one quality -- to say nothing of the others -- completely at odds with what people currently expect of philosophers?"
elegant: Having explained why good philosophers appear useless, Socrates turns to why so many become corrupt -- a corruption that cannot be blamed on philosophy itself. The key trait of the true philosopher is that truth is his guide in all things; without it, he is an impostor. Yet even this quality contradicts popular expectations of philosophers.
summary: Having explained the apparent uselessness of good philosophers, Socrates turns to explaining the corruption of the majority. He recalls that truth-following is the philosopher's essential trait, and notes this already contradicts public expectations.
distilled: The corruption of most philosophers is unavoidable but cannot be blamed on philosophy itself -- it stems from the conflict between the philosophical nature and society.
notes: Speakers: Socrates and Adeimantus -- Transitional passage: from explaining 'uselessness' to explaining 'corruption' -- Truth as the philosopher's leader recalls the earlier portrait (6.3) -- 'Present notions of him' = contemporary Athenian views of philosophers as impractical or dishonest -- Socrates will argue that the very virtues of the philosophical nature make it vulnerable to corruption by society

narrative:
"Certainly," he said.

"And have we not a right to say in his defence, that the true lover of knowledge is always striving after being -- that is his nature; he will not rest in the multiplicity of individuals which is an appearance only, but will go on -- the keen edge will not be blunted, nor the force of his desire abate until he have attained the knowledge of the true nature of every essence by a sympathetic and kindred power in the soul, and by that power drawing near and mingling and becoming incorporate with very being, having begotten mind and truth, he will have knowledge and will live and grow truly, and then, and not till then, will he cease from his travail."
refashioned:
"Certainly," he said.

"And can't we say in the philosopher's defense that the true lover of knowledge is always striving toward being -- that's simply his nature? He won't rest among the multiplicity of particular things, which are mere appearances. His sharp edge won't be dulled, nor will his desire weaken, until he reaches knowledge of the true nature of every essence.

He does this through a sympathetic, kindred power in his soul. Drawing near to being, mingling with it, becoming one with it, he gives birth to mind and truth. Only then does he truly know, truly live, truly grow. And only then -- not a moment before -- does his labor cease."
elegant: The true lover of knowledge strives always toward being itself, never resting among mere appearances. Through a kindred power in the soul, he draws near to true being, mingles with it, becomes one with it, and gives birth to mind and truth. Only then does he truly know, truly live, and find rest from his labor.
summary: Socrates defends the philosopher: the genuine lover of knowledge relentlessly pursues being itself, never settling for appearances. Through a sympathetic power in the soul, he unites with true being and gives birth to mind and truth -- only then finding peace.
distilled: The true philosopher never rests until his soul unites with being itself and gives birth to genuine knowledge and truth.
notes: Speakers: Socrates and Adeimantus -- One of Plato's most lyrical descriptions of philosophical eros -- 'Striving after being' (to on) vs. 'multiplicity of individuals' (appearances/becoming) -- The erotic/generative language ('drawing near,' 'mingling,' 'becoming incorporate,' 'begotten') echoes Diotima's speech in the Symposium (210a-212a) -- 'Sympathetic and kindred power in the soul' = the soul's rational part, which is akin to the Forms -- 'Begotten mind and truth' = genuine knowledge as the offspring of the soul's union with being -- Cf. Symposium: philosophical eros gives birth to wisdom rather than physical children

narrative:
"Nothing," he said, "can be more just than such a description of him."

"And will the love of a lie be any part of a philosopher's nature? Will he not utterly hate a lie?"

"He will."

"And when truth is the captain, we cannot suspect any evil of the band which he leads?"

"Impossible."

"Justice and health of mind will be of the company, and temperance will follow after?"
refashioned:
"Nothing could be a more fitting description of him," he said.

"And would a love of falsehood be any part of a philosopher's nature? Won't he despise lies?"

"He will."

"And when truth leads the way, can we suspect any corruption in the qualities that follow?"

"Impossible."

"Justice and mental health will be in the company, with temperance close behind?"
elegant: The philosopher utterly hates falsehood. When truth leads, justice and health of mind follow naturally, with temperance close behind.
summary: Socrates reaffirms that the philosopher despises falsehood. Where truth leads, justice, mental health, and temperance naturally follow.
distilled: Where truth leads the soul, justice, health of mind, and temperance inevitably follow.
notes: Speakers: Socrates and Adeimantus -- Truth personified as a captain leading a band of virtues -- 'Health of mind' (hugieia) = psychological wholeness, connecting to Book IV's definition of justice as psychic health -- The unity of the virtues: truth, justice, temperance travel together -- This recapitulates the portrait from 6.3-6.5 in compressed form

narrative:
"True," he replied.

"Neither is there any reason why I should again set in array the philosopher's virtues, as you will doubtless remember that courage, magnificence, apprehension, memory, were his natural gifts. And you objected that, although no one could deny what I then said, still, if you leave words and look at facts, the persons who are thus described are some of them manifestly useless, and the greater number utterly depraved; we were then led to enquire into the grounds of these accusations, and have now arrived at the point of asking why are the majority bad, which question of necessity brought us back to the examination and definition of the true philosopher."
refashioned:
"True," he replied.

"There's no need for me to list the philosopher's virtues again -- you'll remember that courage, magnificence, quick comprehension, and strong memory were among his natural gifts. Your objection was that, however unanswerable this sounds in theory, when you look at the facts, some of these supposed philosophers are clearly useless and the majority are thoroughly corrupt.

That objection led us to investigate the basis for these accusations. And now we've arrived at the central question: why are the majority bad? Which has necessarily brought us back to examining and defining what a true philosopher actually is."
elegant: The philosopher's virtues -- courage, magnificence, quick comprehension, memory -- are established. But the objection remains: in practice, most philosophers are corrupt and the best are useless. This forces the investigation back to its core question: why are the majority bad?
summary: Socrates summarizes the argument so far: the philosopher's natural virtues have been catalogued, but the practical objection -- that most philosophers are corrupt and the best useless -- has led them back to the fundamental question of why the majority turn bad.
distilled: The philosopher's virtues are established in theory, but the practical objection -- why are most philosophers corrupt? -- drives the inquiry forward.
notes: Speakers: Socrates and Adeimantus -- This is a structural summary passage, recapping the argument's trajectory -- 'Courage, magnificence, apprehension, memory' = the natural gifts catalogued in 6.3-6.5 -- 'Leave words and look at facts' = Adeimantus' empirical objection from 6.6 -- The dialectical structure: ideal portrait -> objection from reality -> investigation of corruption -- Sets up the crucial argument about how society corrupts philosophical natures (6.17ff)

narrative: "Exactly. And we have next to consider the corruptions of the philosophic nature, why so many are spoiled and so few escape spoiling -- I am speaking of those who were said to be useless but not wicked -- and, when we have done with them, we will speak of the imitators of philosophy, what manner of men are they who aspire after a profession which is above them and of which they are unworthy, and then, by their manifold inconsistencies, bring upon philosophy, and upon all philosophers, that universal reprobation of which we speak."
refashioned:
"Exactly. Now we need to examine how the philosophical nature gets corrupted -- why so many are ruined and so few escape unscathed. I'm talking about those we called useless rather than wicked.

Once we've dealt with that, we'll turn to the imitators of philosophy -- what kind of people aspire to a calling that's above them and that they don't deserve? These are the ones who, through their many contradictions and failures, bring philosophy and all genuine philosophers into the universal disrepute we've been discussing."
elegant: Two questions remain: first, how the philosophical nature is corrupted, explaining why so many are ruined and so few survive intact; second, who are the unworthy imitators of philosophy whose inconsistencies bring universal disgrace upon genuine philosophers.
summary: Socrates outlines two remaining inquiries: why genuinely philosophical natures get corrupted, and how unworthy imitators of philosophy bring disgrace upon the entire discipline through their inconsistencies.
distilled: The investigation must explain both how genuine philosophical natures are corrupted and how unworthy imitators bring philosophy into disrepute.
notes: Speakers: Socrates and Adeimantus -- This passage sets the agenda for the next major section of Book VI -- 'Useless but not wicked' = the best philosophers who withdraw from public life -- 'Imitators of philosophy' = the sophists and pretenders who give philosophy a bad name -- The two-part structure: (1) corruption of genuine natures, (2) invasion by unworthy pretenders -- Anticipates the marriage metaphor of philosophy abandoned by her true suitors (6.30)

narrative:
"What are these corruptions?" he said.

"I will see if I can explain them to you. Every one will admit that a nature having in perfection all the qualities which we required in a philosopher, is a rare plant which is seldom seen among men."

"Rare indeed."

"And what numberless and powerful causes tend to destroy these rare natures!"

"What causes?"

"In the first place there are their own virtues, their courage, temperance, and the rest of them, every one of which praiseworthy qualities (and this is a most singular circumstance) destroys and distracts from philosophy the soul which is the possessor of them."

"That is very singular, he replied."

"Then there are all the ordinary goods of life -- beauty, wealth, strength, rank, and great connections in the State -- you understand the sort of things -- these also have a corrupting and distracting effect."

"I understand; but I should like to know more precisely what you mean about them."

"Grasp the truth as a whole," I said, "and in the right way; you will then have no difficulty in apprehending the preceding remarks, and they will no longer appear strange to you."

"And how am I to do so?" he asked.

"Why," I said, "we know that all germs or seeds, whether vegetable or animal, when they fail to meet with proper nutriment or climate or soil, in proportion to their vigour, are all the more sensitive to the want of a suitable environment, for evil is a greater enemy to what is good than what is not."

"Very true."

"There is reason in supposing that the finest natures, when under alien conditions, receive more injury than the inferior, because the contrast is greater."

"Certainly."

"And may we not say, Adeimantus, that the most gifted minds, when they are ill-educated, become pre-eminently bad? Do not great crimes and the spirit of pure evil spring out of a fulness of nature ruined by education rather than from any inferiority, whereas weak natures are scarcely capable of any very great good or very great evil?"
refashioned:
"What are these corrupting forces?" he asked.

"Let me try to explain. Everyone would agree that a nature possessing all the qualities we require in a philosopher is a rare plant, seldom found among people."

"Rare indeed."

"And countless powerful forces work to destroy these rare natures!"

"What forces?"

"First -- and this is the most paradoxical thing -- their own virtues. Their courage, their temperance, and every other praiseworthy quality actually works to destroy and distract the philosophical soul that possesses them."

"That is very strange," he replied.

"Then there are the ordinary goods of life -- beauty, wealth, physical strength, social rank, powerful connections. You know the kind of thing. These too corrupt and distract."

"I understand the list, but I'd like to know more precisely what you mean."

"Grasp the principle as a whole," I said, "and the details will follow. Think of it this way: all seeds, whether plant or animal, when they don't receive the right nourishment, climate, or soil, suffer in proportion to their vigor. The stronger the seed, the more it suffers from the wrong conditions. Evil is a greater enemy to what is good than to what is mediocre."

"Very true."

"So it makes sense that the finest natures, placed in hostile conditions, suffer more damage than inferior ones -- precisely because the gap between what they are and what they could be is so much greater."

"Certainly."

"Then can't we say, Adeimantus, that the most gifted minds, when badly educated, become exceptionally bad? Don't the greatest crimes and the purest evil spring from a powerful nature ruined by bad upbringing, rather than from inherent weakness? Weak natures are barely capable of great good or great evil."
elegant: The philosophical nature is rare, and paradoxically, its very virtues -- courage, temperance, and the rest -- along with worldly advantages like beauty and wealth, work to corrupt it. Like a vigorous seed that suffers most from poor soil, the strongest natures are most damaged by hostile conditions. The most gifted minds, when badly educated, become the most dangerous -- for great evil springs from powerful natures ruined, not from weak ones.
summary: Socrates reveals the paradox of philosophical corruption: the philosopher's own virtues and worldly advantages are what destroy him. Like vigorous seeds needing the right soil, the strongest natures suffer most from bad conditions. The most gifted minds, when corrupted, produce the greatest evil.
distilled: The philosopher's own virtues and advantages paradoxically become the instruments of his corruption -- the finest natures, badly nurtured, produce the greatest evil.
notes: Speakers: Socrates and Adeimantus -- The paradox of virtue becoming corruption is central to Book VI's argument -- The seed/plant analogy: philosophical natures need the right 'soil' (education, political environment) -- 'Evil is a greater enemy to what is good than what is not' = a principle of proportional vulnerability -- This explains why democracies produce both the greatest tyrants and the greatest philosophers -- Cf. Book VIII-IX: the tyrant often emerges from the most gifted nature -- The argument anticipates Alcibiades, the gifted youth corrupted by Athens (though not named here)

narrative:
"There I think that you are right."

"And our philosopher follows the same analogy -- he is like a plant which, having proper nurture, must necessarily grow and mature into all virtue, but, if sown and planted in an alien soil, becomes the most noxious of all weeds, unless he be preserved by some divine power. Do you really think, as people so often say, that our youth are corrupted by Sophists, or that private teachers of the art corrupt them in any degree worth speaking of? Are not the public who say these things the greatest of all Sophists? And do they not educate to perfection young and old, men and women alike, and fashion them after their own hearts?"
refashioned:
"I think you're right about that."

"And our philosopher follows the same pattern -- he's like a plant that, with proper care, will inevitably grow into full virtue. But plant him in hostile soil, and he becomes the most poisonous of weeds -- unless some divine power preserves him.

And do you really believe, as people constantly say, that it's the Sophists who corrupt the young? That private teachers do any corruption worth mentioning? Aren't the very people who make this complaint the greatest Sophists of all? Isn't the public itself the ultimate educator -- shaping young and old, men and women alike, molding them into its own image?"
elegant: The philosopher is like a plant: properly nurtured, he grows into full virtue; planted in hostile soil, he becomes the most noxious weed. The real corrupters are not the Sophists but the public itself -- the greatest Sophist of all -- which educates everyone, young and old, in its own image.
summary: Socrates extends the plant analogy: the philosopher becomes virtuous with proper nurture but turns into the worst weed in hostile conditions. He provocatively argues that the real corrupters of youth are not the Sophists but the general public, which is the greatest Sophist of all.
distilled: The public itself is the greatest Sophist -- not private teachers, but society at large corrupts philosophical natures by molding everyone in its own image.
notes: Speakers: Socrates and Adeimantus -- 'Corrupted by Sophists' = a common Athenian complaint, notably the charge against Socrates himself (Apology 19d-20c) -- Socrates reverses the charge: the demos is the real Sophist -- 'Divine power' (theia moira) = the rare cases where a philosophical nature survives corruption -- 'Fashion them after their own hearts' = social conformity as the deepest form of education -- This is a radical critique of democratic culture: the majority's values are the true curriculum -- Cf. Gorgias 463a-466a on rhetoric as the art of flattering the crowd

narrative:
"When is this accomplished?" he said.

"When they meet together, and the world sits down at an assembly, or in a court of law, or a theatre, or a camp, or in any other popular resort, and there is a great uproar, and they praise some things which are being said or done, and blame other things, equally exaggerating both, shouting and clapping their hands, and the echo of the rocks and the place in which they are assembled redoubles the sound of the praise or blame -- at such a time will not a young man's heart, as they say, leap within him? Will any private training enable him to stand firm against the overwhelming flood of popular opinion? or will he be carried away by the stream? Will he not have the notions of good and evil which the public in general have -- he will do as they do, and as they are, such will he be?"
refashioned:
"When does this happen?" he asked.

"Whenever people gather together -- in an assembly, a courtroom, a theater, a military camp, or any public gathering. There's a tremendous uproar as they praise certain things and condemn others, exaggerating in both directions, shouting and clapping while the echo of the walls amplifies everything into a deafening roar.

At such a moment, won't a young person's heart race? Can any private education hold firm against that overwhelming flood of popular opinion, or will he be swept away? Won't he end up adopting whatever notions of good and evil the crowd holds? He'll do as they do, and become what they are."
elegant: The public educates through the overwhelming force of collective emotion -- in assemblies, courts, theaters, and camps, the crowd's praise and blame drown out all private training. No young person can stand firm against this flood; he will inevitably adopt the crowd's values and become what they are.
summary: Socrates describes how public gatherings -- assemblies, courts, theaters -- create an overwhelming tide of collective emotion that drowns out any private philosophical training. No young person can resist this flood; they inevitably absorb the crowd's values.
distilled: The overwhelming emotional force of public assemblies drowns out private philosophical education, making the young adopt whatever the crowd values.
notes: Speakers: Socrates and Adeimantus -- A vivid description of crowd psychology and social pressure in Athenian democracy -- The list of venues (assembly, court, theatre, camp) = all major sites of Athenian public life -- 'Echo of the rocks' = the physical amplification of crowd noise, symbolizing how groupthink is reinforced -- The 'flood' or 'stream' metaphor for popular opinion (cf. the stream metaphor for desire in 6.4) -- This anticipates modern crowd psychology (cf. Gustave Le Bon, The Crowd) -- The young person's 'heart leaping' = the emotional pull of belonging to the group

narrative:
"Yes, Socrates; necessity will compel him."

"And yet," I said, "there is a still greater necessity, which has not been mentioned."

"What is that?"

"The gentle force of attainder or confiscation or death which, as you are aware, these new Sophists and educators who are the public, apply when their words are powerless."

"Indeed they do; and in right good earnest."

"Now what opinion of any other Sophist, or of any private person, can be expected to overcome in such an unequal contest?"
refashioned:
"Yes, Socrates -- he'll be forced to conform."

"And yet," I said, "there's an even greater compulsion we haven't mentioned."

"What's that?"

"The 'gentle' force of legal penalties, property seizure, and execution -- which the public, this great Sophist and educator, applies when its words aren't persuasive enough."

"Indeed they do -- and they mean it."

"So what private teacher or individual Sophist can hope to prevail in such a lopsided contest?"
elegant: Beyond social pressure, the public enforces conformity through legal penalties, confiscation, and death. When persuasion fails, force follows. No private teacher can compete with this power.
summary: Socrates adds that beyond social pressure, the public enforces its values through legal punishments, confiscation, and death. No private Sophist or teacher can compete against such overwhelming coercive power.
distilled: The public enforces its education not only through social pressure but through the ultimate sanctions of punishment and death -- no private teacher can compete.
notes: Speakers: Socrates and Adeimantus -- 'Gentle force' is deeply ironic -- attainder, confiscation, and death are anything but gentle -- 'These new Sophists...who are the public' = the crowd as the real educator, using both persuasion and coercion -- A clear allusion to Socrates' own fate: the Athenian public executed him for 'corrupting the youth' -- The argument: the real corruption comes from the same public that accuses philosophers of corruption -- Cf. Apology 24b-26b: the trial of Socrates on charges of corrupting youth

narrative:
"None," he replied.

"No, indeed," I said, "even to make the attempt is a great piece of folly; there neither is, nor has been, nor is ever likely to be, any different type of character which has had no other training in virtue but that which is supplied by public opinion -- I speak, my friend, of human virtue only; what is more than human, as the proverb says, is not included: for I would not have you ignorant that, in the present evil state of governments, whatever is saved and comes to good is saved by the power of God, as we may truly say."

"I quite assent," he replied.

"Then let me crave your assent also to a further observation."
refashioned:
"None," he replied.

"No indeed," I said. "Even attempting to resist is folly. There has never been, is not now, and never will be a character formed in virtue by anything other than public opinion -- at least as far as human virtue goes. The exception, as the proverb says, is whatever is more than human. And I want to be clear: in the present corrupt state of government, anything that is saved and turns out well is saved, as we may truly say, by divine intervention alone."

"I completely agree," he replied.

"Then let me ask you to consider one more point."
elegant: No character can be formed in virtue against the overwhelming force of public opinion -- only divine intervention can preserve anyone in the current corrupt political order. Human virtue shaped independently of the crowd is essentially impossible.
summary: Socrates declares that no human character can develop virtue independently of public opinion's overwhelming influence. In the present corrupt political order, only divine power can preserve a philosophical nature.
distilled: Under current corrupt governments, no human virtue can develop independently of public opinion -- only divine intervention can save a philosophical nature.
notes: Speakers: Socrates and Adeimantus -- 'What is more than human' = a proverbial exception for divine or superhuman cases -- 'Saved by the power of God' (theia moira) = recurring Platonic concept of divine dispensation -- This echoes Socrates' own daimonion (inner divine sign) mentioned in the Apology -- The pessimism about existing governments anticipates the claim that no current state is suitable for philosophy (6.35) -- A rare moment of near-despair in the Republic's argument

narrative:
"What are you going to say?"

"Why, that all those mercenary individuals, whom the many call Sophists and whom they deem to be their adversaries, do, in fact, teach nothing but the opinion of the many, that is to say, the opinions of their assemblies; and this is their wisdom. I might compare them to a man who should study the tempers and desires of a mighty strong beast who is fed by him -- he would learn how to approach and handle him, also at what times and from what causes he is dangerous or the reverse, and what is the meaning of his several cries, and by what sounds, when another utters them, he is soothed or infuriated; and you may suppose further, that when, by continually attending upon him, he has become perfect in all this, he calls his knowledge wisdom, and makes of it a system or art, which he proceeds to teach, although he has no real notion of what he means by the principles or passions of which he is speaking, but calls this honourable and that dishonourable, or good or evil, or just or unjust, all in accordance with the tastes and tempers of the great brute. Good he pronounces to be that in which the beast delights and evil to be that which he dislikes; and he can give no other account of them except that the just and noble are the necessary, having never himself seen, and having no power of explaining to others the nature of either, or the difference between them, which is immense. By heaven, would not such an one be a rare educator?"
refashioned:
"What's your point?"

"This: all those hired intellectuals the public calls Sophists -- the ones they think are their opponents -- actually teach nothing but the public's own opinions. The opinions of their assemblies: that's the Sophists' wisdom.

Imagine someone who tends a large, powerful beast. He studies its moods and appetites -- learning when to approach, what makes it dangerous or calm, what its different cries mean, and which sounds soothe or enrage it. After years of careful observation, he masters all this, calls it 'wisdom,' systematizes it, and starts teaching it.

But he has no actual understanding of the principles behind the beast's reactions. He simply labels whatever pleases the beast 'good' and 'honorable,' and whatever displeases it 'bad' and 'shameful.' He calls what is necessary 'just' and 'noble.' He has never seen the true nature of good or justice, cannot explain the vast difference between genuine goodness and mere crowd-pleasing, and has no capacity to teach that difference to anyone else.

Wouldn't such a person be a remarkable educator?"
elegant: The Sophists teach nothing but the crowd's own opinions, repackaged as wisdom. They are like a keeper who studies a powerful beast's moods -- learning what soothes or enrages it -- then calls this knowledge 'wisdom' and teaches it. He labels whatever pleases the beast 'good' and whatever displeases it 'evil,' having never glimpsed the true nature of either. The difference between genuine goodness and crowd-pleasing is immense, but he cannot see it.
summary: Socrates argues that Sophists merely systematize and teach back the crowd's own opinions. He compares them to a beast-keeper who studies an animal's moods and calls this 'wisdom,' labeling whatever pleases the beast 'good' without any understanding of genuine goodness.
distilled: The Sophists' wisdom is merely a systematized study of the crowd's appetites -- calling whatever pleases the beast 'good' without knowing what goodness truly is.
notes: Speakers: Socrates and Adeimantus -- The Great Beast analogy: one of Plato's most powerful images for democratic culture -- The beast = the demos/public -- The beast-keeper = the Sophist/politician who studies crowd psychology -- 'Calls his knowledge wisdom' = the Sophist's fundamental error: confusing crowd management with genuine knowledge -- The distinction between the necessary/conventional and the genuinely good anticipates the Form of the Good discussion -- Cf. Gorgias 463a-465e: rhetoric as the 'knack' (empeiria) of flattering the crowd vs. genuine political art -- 'The just and noble are the necessary' = the keeper conflates what the beast must tolerate with what is truly good

narrative:
"Indeed, he would."

"And in what way does he who thinks that wisdom is the discernment of the tempers and tastes of the motley multitude, whether in painting or music, or, finally, in politics, differ from him whom I have been describing? For when a man consorts with the many, and exhibits to them his poem or other work of art or the service which he has done the State, making them his judges when he is not obliged, the so-called necessity of Diomede will oblige him to produce whatever they praise. And yet the reasons are utterly ludicrous which they give in confirmation of their own notions about the honourable and good. Did you ever hear any of them which were not?"
refashioned:
"A remarkable educator indeed."

"And how is someone who thinks wisdom means understanding the tastes and moods of the diverse crowd -- whether in painting, music, or politics -- any different from the beast-keeper I just described?

When someone takes his work -- a poem, a piece of art, or a political achievement -- before the crowd and makes them his judges when he doesn't have to, a kind of iron necessity forces him to produce whatever they praise. And the reasons the crowd gives for why something is 'honorable' or 'good'? They're utterly absurd. Have you ever heard any that weren't?"
elegant: Anyone who equates wisdom with reading the crowd's tastes -- in art, music, or politics -- is no different from the beast-keeper. Making the crowd your judge creates an iron compulsion to produce whatever they praise, and their reasons for calling things 'good' or 'honorable' are always absurd.
summary: Socrates extends the beast-keeper analogy: anyone in art, music, or politics who makes the crowd their judge becomes enslaved to public taste. The compulsion to produce what the crowd praises is inescapable, and the crowd's justifications for their values are always absurd.
distilled: Making the crowd your judge in art or politics enslaves you to their taste -- and their reasons for what they call 'good' are always absurd.
notes: Speakers: Socrates and Adeimantus -- 'Necessity of Diomede' = a Greek proverb meaning an absolute, inescapable compulsion (from Diomedes who compelled his guests) -- The argument extends from politics to all arts: painting, music, poetry -- This critiques democratic aesthetics as well as democratic politics -- Cf. Ion and Republic Book X on poets as imitators who please crowds -- The argument anticipates modern critiques of market-driven art and populist politics -- 'Consorts with the many' = the artist/politician who makes popular approval his standard

narrative:
"No, nor am I likely to hear."

"You recognise the truth of what I have been saying? Then let me ask you to consider further whether the world will ever be induced to believe in the existence of absolute beauty rather than of the many beautiful, or of the absolute in each kind rather than of the many in each kind?"
refashioned:
"No, and I don't expect to."

"So you see the truth of what I'm saying. Then consider this further question: will the world ever be brought to believe in absolute beauty -- as opposed to the many beautiful things? Or in the absolute form of anything, rather than the many particular instances?"
elegant: The crowd will never be brought to believe in absolute beauty rather than many beautiful things, or in the absolute form of anything rather than its many particular instances.
summary: Socrates asks whether the general public can ever be brought to believe in absolute Forms -- such as absolute beauty -- rather than remaining focused on the many particular beautiful things.
distilled: The multitude will never believe in absolute beauty or any Form -- they can only see the many particulars.
notes: Speakers: Socrates and Adeimantus -- 'Absolute beauty' vs. 'the many beautiful' = the Form of Beauty vs. particular beautiful things -- This restates the fundamental metaphysical divide from Book V (475e-476d) -- The crowd is constitutionally unable to be philosophical because they cannot grasp universals -- This has implications for the Form of the Good discussion to come -- The question is rhetorical: the answer is clearly 'no'

narrative:
"Certainly not."

"Then the world cannot possibly be a philosopher?"

"Impossible."

"And therefore philosophers must inevitably fall under the censure of the world?"

"They must."

"And of individuals who consort with the mob and seek to please them?"

"That is evident."

"Then, do you see any way in which the philosopher can be preserved in his calling to the end? and remember what we were saying of him, that he was to have quickness and memory and courage and magnificence -- these were admitted by us to be the true philosopher's gifts."

"Yes."

"Will not such an one from his early childhood be in all things first among all, especially if his bodily endowments are like his mental ones?"

"Certainly," he said.

"And his friends and fellow-citizens will want to use him as he gets older for their own purposes?"

"No question."

"Falling at his feet, they will make requests to him and do him honour and flatter him, because they want to get into their hands now, the power which he will one day possess."
refashioned:
"Certainly not."

"Then the world can never be philosophical?"

"Impossible."

"So philosophers will inevitably face the world's disapproval?"

"They must."

"Along with disapproval from individuals who court the mob and try to please them?"

"Obviously."

"Then can you see any way to preserve the philosopher in his calling? Remember, we said his gifts were quickness, strong memory, courage, and magnanimity."

"Yes."

"Won't someone with these qualities stand out from earliest childhood, especially if his physical qualities match his mental ones?"

"Certainly," he said.

"And as he grows older, won't his friends and fellow citizens want to use him for their own purposes?"

"No question."

"They'll fall at his feet, making requests, heaping honors and flattery on him -- because they want to secure for themselves the power he will one day wield."
elegant: The world can never be philosophical, so philosophers will always face public censure. The gifted philosophical youth -- quick, courageous, magnanimous -- will stand out from childhood, and society will immediately try to exploit him, heaping flattery and honors to capture his future power for their own purposes.
summary: Since the crowd can never be philosophical, philosophers will always be censured. The young person with a philosophical nature -- gifted, courageous, magnanimous -- will be targeted early by those who want to exploit his talents, receiving flattery and honors designed to capture his future influence.
distilled: Society inevitably tries to co-opt the gifted philosophical youth through flattery and honor, seeking to capture his future power before philosophy can shape him.
notes: Speakers: Socrates and Adeimantus -- The corruption of the philosophical youth begins: society targets his gifts -- 'Quickness and memory and courage and magnificence' = the very virtues catalogued in 6.3-6.5, now shown as vulnerabilities -- The pattern described fits Alcibiades perfectly: brilliant, beautiful, courted by Athenian politicians from youth -- 'Power which he will one day possess' = political influence that follows from natural gifts -- The argument: the philosopher's gifts make him a target for political exploitation -- Cf. Alcibiades I (if genuine) for Socrates' attempt to save such a youth

narrative:
"That often happens," he said.

"And what will a man such as he be likely to do under such circumstances, especially if he be a citizen of a great city, rich and noble, and a tall proper youth? Will he not be full of boundless aspirations, and fancy himself able to manage the affairs of Hellenes and of barbarians, and having got such notions into his head will he not dilate and elevate himself in the fulness of vain pomp and senseless pride?"

"To be sure he will."

"Now, when he is in this state of mind, if some one gently comes to him and tells him that he is a fool and must get understanding, which can only be got by slaving for it, do you think that, under such adverse circumstances, he will be easily induced to listen?"
refashioned:
"That happens all the time," he said.

"And what will such a person do in those circumstances -- especially if he's a citizen of a great city, rich, well-born, and physically impressive? Won't he swell with limitless ambition, convinced he can manage the affairs of Greeks and non-Greeks alike? Won't he inflate himself with empty grandeur and senseless pride?"

"Absolutely."

"Now, while he's in this state of mind, imagine someone approaches him gently and tells him he's a fool -- that real understanding can only be won through hard, grinding work. Do you think, given everything working against it, he'll be easily persuaded to listen?"
elegant: The gifted youth in a great city, rich and well-born, swells with boundless ambition and empty pride, convinced he can manage the world's affairs. When someone gently tells him he's a fool who must slave for real understanding, such a youth will not easily be persuaded to listen.
summary: Socrates describes how a gifted young person in a great city becomes inflated with ambition and pride through flattery. When someone tries to tell him he needs to work hard for genuine understanding, he is unlikely to listen.
distilled: The gifted youth, inflated by flattery and ambition, will not listen when told he must slave for genuine understanding.
notes: Speakers: Socrates and Adeimantus -- 'A citizen of a great city, rich and noble, and a tall proper youth' = a clear portrait of Alcibiades, though unnamed -- 'Affairs of Hellenes and barbarians' = pan-Hellenic and imperial ambitions, characteristic of Athenian political culture -- 'Vain pomp and senseless pride' = hubris, the classic Greek vice -- The 'someone who gently comes' = a figure like Socrates himself, who tried to redirect Alcibiades toward philosophy -- 'Slaving for understanding' = the demanding nature of genuine philosophical education -- Cf. Alcibiades I 104a-106a, where Socrates confronts Alcibiades about his ambitions

narrative:
"Far otherwise."

"And even if there be some one who through inherent goodness or natural reasonableness has had his eyes opened a little and is humbled and taken captive by philosophy, how will his friends behave when they think that they are likely to lose the advantage which they were hoping to reap from his companionship? Will they not do and say anything to prevent him from yielding to his better nature and to render his teacher powerless, using to this end private intrigues as well as public prosecutions?"
refashioned:
"Far from it."

"And even if someone -- through inherent goodness or natural open-mindedness -- does have his eyes opened a little, is humbled, and begins to be drawn toward philosophy, how will his friends and associates react? When they realize they might lose the political advantages they expected to gain from his friendship, won't they do and say anything to stop him? They'll try to prevent him from following his better nature and to neutralize his teacher -- using private scheming and public prosecution alike."
