elegant: Even if a gifted youth is drawn toward philosophy through inherent goodness, his friends and political allies will fight to prevent it -- using both private intrigue and public prosecution -- to preserve the advantages they expect to gain from his power.
summary: Even when a gifted youth begins to turn toward philosophy, his friends and political allies will use every means -- private intrigues and public prosecutions -- to prevent it, since they stand to lose the political advantages they expected from his companionship.
distilled: Society fights to prevent the gifted youth from turning to philosophy, using both private scheming and public prosecution to neutralize his philosophical teachers.
notes: Speakers: Socrates and Adeimantus -- 'Public prosecutions' = a direct allusion to Socrates' own trial, where he was prosecuted for allegedly corrupting the youth -- 'Private intrigues' = behind-the-scenes pressure from those who benefited from the youth's political ambitions -- The dynamic described: society actively resists philosophical conversion of its most talented members -- Cf. Apology 23d-24a: Socrates' prosecutors acted partly from personal resentment -- The 'teacher' being rendered powerless = Socrates' own fate

narrative:
"There can be no doubt of it."

"And how can one who is thus circumstanced ever become a philosopher?"

"Impossible."

"Then were we not right in saying that even the very qualities which make a man a philosopher may, if he be ill-educated, divert him from philosophy, no less than riches and their accompaniments and the other so-called goods of life?"
refashioned:
"There's no doubt about it."

"And how can someone in those circumstances ever become a philosopher?"

"It's impossible."

"Then weren't we right to say that the very qualities that make someone a natural philosopher -- if he's badly educated -- can actually divert him away from philosophy? And that riches, social status, and all the other supposed goods of life work the same way?"
elegant: The very qualities that make someone a natural philosopher, combined with bad education and worldly advantages, become the forces that divert him from philosophy. The philosopher's own gifts become his undoing.
summary: Socrates confirms the paradox: the very qualities that constitute a philosophical nature -- combined with poor education and worldly advantages -- are precisely what drive the gifted person away from philosophy.
distilled: The philosopher's own natural gifts, corrupted by bad education and worldly success, become the very forces that divert him from philosophy.
notes: Speakers: Socrates and Adeimantus -- This completes the paradox introduced in 6.17: the philosopher's virtues are his vulnerabilities -- 'So-called goods of life' = wealth, beauty, strength, connections (6.17) -- The argument structure: gifts + wrong environment = corruption away from philosophy -- Cf. Book VIII: the decline of constitutions follows a similar logic of corruption through excess -- The conclusion is that philosophical corruption is systematic, not accidental

narrative:
"We were quite right."

"Thus, my excellent friend, is brought about all that ruin and failure which I have been describing of the natures best adapted to the best of all pursuits; they are natures which we maintain to be rare at any time; this being the class out of which come the men who are the authors of the greatest evil to States and individuals; and also of the greatest good when the tide carries them in that direction; but a small man never was the doer of any great thing either to individuals or to States."
refashioned:
"We were exactly right."

"And so, my friend, this is how the ruin and failure I've been describing comes about -- the destruction of natures best suited for the highest pursuit. These natures are rare in any era. From this class come the people who cause the greatest evil to states and individuals -- and also the greatest good, when the current carries them that way. But a small, mediocre person never accomplished anything truly great, for good or for evil."
elegant: The ruin of the finest natures, best suited for philosophy, produces both the greatest evil and the greatest good. A mediocre person never accomplishes anything truly significant -- only great natures have the capacity for great impact, whether for destruction or salvation.
summary: Socrates concludes that the ruin of the finest philosophical natures is what produces history's greatest villains -- but also its greatest benefactors. Mediocre people never achieve anything of great significance, for good or ill.
distilled: Great natures produce both the greatest good and the greatest evil -- mediocre natures never produce anything of significance.
notes: Speakers: Socrates and Adeimantus -- 'A small man never was the doer of any great thing' = an important Platonic principle -- The same nature that could become a philosopher-king becomes a tyrant when corrupted -- Cf. Book VIII-IX: the tyrant emerges from the same gifted class as the philosopher -- Historical examples: Alcibiades (great gifts, great destruction) vs. Socrates (great gifts, great good) -- This principle applies to Plato's theory of political change: transformative leaders come from the same rare class

narrative:
"That is most true," he said.

"And so philosophy is left desolate, with her marriage rite incomplete: for her own have fallen away and forsaken her, and while they are leading a false and unbecoming life, other unworthy persons, seeing that she has no kinsmen to be her protectors, enter in and dishonour her; and fasten upon her the reproaches which, as you say, her reprovers utter, who affirm of her votaries that some are good for nothing, and that the greater number deserve the severest punishment."
refashioned:
"That is absolutely true," he said.

"And so philosophy is left desolate, her marriage ceremony incomplete. Her true suitors have fallen away and abandoned her to live false, unworthy lives. Meanwhile, other unworthy people -- seeing that she has no family left to protect her -- move in and dishonor her. They fasten onto her all the reproaches you mentioned: that her followers are good for nothing, and the majority deserve the harshest punishment."
elegant: Philosophy is left desolate, her marriage incomplete. Her true suitors have abandoned her for false lives, and unworthy pretenders -- seeing her unprotected -- move in, dishonor her, and bring upon her the reproach that her followers are either useless or deserving of punishment.
summary: Socrates personifies philosophy as a woman abandoned by her rightful suitors, left vulnerable to unworthy pretenders who dishonor her and bring upon her the very reproaches that give philosophy its bad reputation.
distilled: Philosophy is abandoned by her true suitors and dishonored by unworthy pretenders, who bring upon her the reproaches that damage her reputation.
notes: Speakers: Socrates and Adeimantus -- Philosophy personified as a bride or noblewoman (a striking gendered metaphor) -- 'Marriage rite incomplete' = the union of philosophy with its proper practitioners never consummated -- 'Her own have fallen away' = the genuinely gifted who were corrupted -- 'Unworthy persons' = the pretenders and sophists who fill the vacuum -- This answers Adeimantus' original objection (6.6): philosophy's bad reputation comes from its false practitioners, not from philosophy itself -- The marriage metaphor anticipates the 'bald tinker' image (6.32)

narrative:
"That is certainly what people say."

"Yes; and what else would you expect," I said, "when you think of the puny creatures who, seeing this land open to them -- a land well stocked with fair names and showy titles -- like prisoners running out of prison into a sanctuary, take a leap out of their trades into philosophy; those who do so being probably the cleverest hands at their own miserable crafts? For, although philosophy be in this evil case, still there remains a dignity about her which is not to be found in the arts. And many are thus attracted by her whose natures are imperfect and whose souls are maimed and disfigured by their meannesses, as their bodies are by their trades and crafts. Is not this unavoidable?"
refashioned:
"That's certainly what people say."

"And what else would you expect," I said, "when you consider the kind of people who see this territory wide open -- a field full of impressive titles and distinguished names -- and leap from their petty trades into philosophy like prisoners escaping into a sanctuary?

These are probably the cleverest operators in their own small-time professions. For despite philosophy's fallen state, she still carries a dignity that no mere trade or craft possesses. So she attracts people with imperfect natures -- souls stunted and deformed by their small-mindedness, just as their bodies are by their manual labor. Isn't this inevitable?"
elegant: Philosophy, despite her fallen state, retains a dignity that trades and crafts lack. This attracts the wrong people -- clever operators from petty professions who leap into philosophy like prisoners into sanctuary, bringing with them souls deformed by small-mindedness, just as their bodies are shaped by manual labor.
summary: Despite philosophy's degraded condition, she retains a dignity that attracts the wrong people -- clever but small-minded tradesmen who leap into philosophy, bringing deformed souls with them, drawn by her prestigious titles and reputation.
distilled: Philosophy's residual dignity attracts the wrong people -- clever tradesmen with small souls who are drawn to her prestigious titles rather than her truth.
notes: Speakers: Socrates and Adeimantus -- 'Prisoners running into a sanctuary' = the asylum metaphor -- philosophy as a refuge from low-status work -- The social prejudice against manual labor reflects aristocratic Greek values -- 'Fair names and showy titles' = the philosopher's reputation for wisdom -- The critique targets social climbers who use philosophy for status -- 'Souls maimed and disfigured by their meannesses' = the deformation of character by petty concerns -- Cf. the 'bald tinker' metaphor that follows (6.32)

narrative:
"Yes."

"Are they not exactly like a bald little tinker who has just got out of durance and come into a fortune; he takes a bath and puts on a new coat, and is decked out as a bridegroom going to marry his master's daughter, who is left poor and desolate?"

"A most exact parallel."

"What will be the issue of such marriages? Will they not be vile and bastard?"

"There can be no question of it."

"And when persons who are unworthy of education approach philosophy and make an alliance with her who is a rank above them what sort of ideas and opinions are likely to be generated? Will they not be sophisms captivating to the ear, having nothing in them genuine, or worthy of or akin to true wisdom?"
refashioned:
"Yes."

"Aren't they exactly like a bald little tinker who's just been released from prison and come into a fortune? He takes a bath, puts on fine clothes, and gets himself up as a bridegroom to marry his former master's daughter -- who's been left poor and abandoned."

"That's a perfect parallel."

"And what will come of such marriages? Won't the offspring be base and illegitimate?"

"Without question."

"And when people unworthy of real education approach philosophy -- a discipline far above them -- and form an alliance with her, what kind of ideas and opinions will they produce? Won't they be clever-sounding sophisms with nothing genuine in them -- nothing worthy of or akin to true wisdom?"
elegant: The unworthy pretender to philosophy is like a bald tinker who inherits a fortune and dresses up to marry his abandoned master's daughter. Such unions produce only base, illegitimate offspring -- sophisms that sound clever but contain nothing genuine or akin to true wisdom.
summary: Socrates compares unworthy practitioners of philosophy to a freed tinker who marries above his station. Such unions produce only illegitimate ideas -- clever-sounding sophisms with no genuine wisdom in them.
distilled: When unworthy people claim philosophy, they produce only sophisticated-sounding nonsense -- the illegitimate offspring of a mismatched union.
notes: Speakers: Socrates and Adeimantus -- The 'bald tinker' metaphor continues the marriage/bride imagery of 6.30 -- Philosophy = the abandoned noblewoman; the pretender = the social-climbing tinker -- 'Vile and bastard' offspring = the sophisms produced by false philosophy -- This explains why philosophy has a bad reputation: its public face is the products of unworthy practitioners -- Cf. Theaetetus 150c-151d: Socrates as midwife distinguishing genuine philosophical offspring from 'wind eggs'

narrative:
"No doubt," he said.

"Then, Adeimantus," I said, "the worthy disciples of philosophy will be but a small remnant: perchance some noble and well-educated person, detained by exile in her service, who in the absence of corrupting influences remains devoted to her; or some lofty soul born in a mean city, the politics of which he contemns and neglects; and there may be a gifted few who leave the arts, which they justly despise, and come to her; -- or peradventure there are some who are restrained by our friend Theages' bridle; for everything in the life of Theages conspired to divert him from philosophy; but ill-health kept him away from politics. My own case of the internal sign is hardly worth mentioning, for rarely, if ever, has such a monitor been given to any other man.

Those who belong to this small class have tasted how sweet and blessed a possession philosophy is, and have also seen enough of the madness of the multitude; and they know that no politician is honest, nor is there any champion of justice at whose side they may fight and be saved. Such an one may be compared to a man who has fallen among wild beasts -- he will not join in the wickedness of his fellows, but neither is he able singly to resist all their fierce natures, and therefore seeing that he would be of no use to the State or to his friends, and reflecting that he would have to throw away his life without doing any good either to himself or others, he holds his peace, and goes his own way. He is like one who, in the storm of dust and sleet which the driving wind hurries along, retires under the shelter of a wall; and seeing the rest of mankind full of wickedness, he is content, if only he can live his own life and be pure from evil or unrighteousness, and depart in peace and good-will, with bright hopes."
refashioned:
"No doubt," he said.

"So, Adeimantus, the worthy disciples of philosophy will be just a small remnant. Perhaps someone noble and well-educated who is detained in philosophy's service by exile, and without corrupting influences remains devoted to her. Or a great soul born in a small city whose politics he scorns and ignores. Or a gifted few who leave other professions they rightly despise and come to philosophy. Or perhaps someone restrained by what we might call 'Theages' bridle' -- everything in Theages' life conspired to pull him from philosophy, but chronic illness kept him out of politics. My own case -- my inner divine sign -- is hardly worth mentioning, since such a monitor has rarely, if ever, been given to anyone else.

Those in this small class have tasted how sweet and blessed a thing philosophy is. They've also seen enough of the crowd's madness to know that no politician is honest, and there is no champion of justice at whose side they might fight and survive.

Such a person is like someone who has fallen among wild beasts. He won't join in their savagery, but he can't resist them all alone either. Seeing that he'd be useless to the state or to his friends -- that he'd have to throw his life away without doing anyone any good -- he keeps quiet and goes his own way.

He's like a traveler caught in a storm of dust and driving rain who takes shelter behind a wall. Watching the rest of humanity drown in wickedness, he is content if he can live his own life free from injustice, and depart in peace and goodwill, with bright hopes."
elegant: The true disciples of philosophy are a tiny remnant -- exiles, souls born in small cities, a gifted few who abandon other professions, or those kept from politics by illness or divine signs. They have tasted philosophy's sweetness and seen the crowd's madness. Knowing no politician is honest and no champion of justice exists, they are like travelers sheltering behind a wall during a storm, content to live their own lives free from injustice, departing in peace with bright hopes.
summary: Socrates describes the tiny remnant of genuine philosophers: exiles, great souls in small cities, and a few protected by circumstance. Having tasted philosophy and witnessed the crowd's madness, they withdraw from public life like travelers sheltering from a storm, content to live justly and die in peace.
distilled: The few genuine philosophers, knowing the madness of the crowd and the corruption of politics, withdraw like storm-sheltering travelers -- content to live justly and depart in peace.
notes: Speakers: Socrates and Adeimantus -- 'Theages' bridle' refers to Theages, a would-be politician kept from politics by illness (cf. the dialogue Theages, possibly spurious) -- 'My own case of the internal sign' = Socrates' daimonion, his famous inner voice that warned him away from certain actions (Apology 31c-d) -- The 'man among wild beasts' = the philosopher in a corrupt society -- The 'wall in the storm' = one of Book VI's most beautiful images for philosophical withdrawal -- This passage is deeply autobiographical, reflecting Socrates' own withdrawal from Athenian politics (Apology 31c-32e) -- 'Bright hopes' (lampras elpidas) = hope for a good afterlife, connecting to the myth of Er (Book X) -- The passage is also melancholic: the philosopher achieves only private virtue, not the public good he could accomplish in a just state

narrative:
"Yes," he said, "and he will have done a great work before he departs."

"A great work -- yes; but not the greatest, unless he find a State suitable to him; for in a State which is suitable to him, he will have a larger growth and be the saviour of his country, as well as of himself."

"The causes why philosophy is in such an evil name have now been sufficiently explained: the injustice of the charges against her has been shown -- is there anything more which you wish to say?"

"Nothing more on that subject," he replied; "but I should like to know which of the governments now existing is in your opinion the one adapted to her."
refashioned:
"Yes," he said, "and he will have accomplished something great before he goes."

"A great thing, yes -- but not the greatest. For if he found a state suited to him, he would grow to his full stature and become the savior of his country as well as of himself."

"The reasons philosophy has such a bad name have now been sufficiently explained. The injustice of the charges against her has been demonstrated. Is there anything more you want to say on this subject?"

"Nothing more about that," he replied. "But I'd like to know: which of the existing governments do you think is suited to philosophy?"
elegant: The philosopher who withdraws does something great -- but not the greatest. In a suitable state, he would grow to full stature and save his country along with himself. The causes of philosophy's bad reputation have been explained. The question now is: which existing government suits philosophy?
summary: Adeimantus acknowledges the withdrawn philosopher's achievement, but Socrates insists it falls short of what would be possible in a suitable state, where the philosopher could save both himself and his country. The causes of philosophy's bad reputation have been explained; Adeimantus asks which existing government suits philosophy.
distilled: The withdrawn philosopher achieves private virtue, but only a suitable state would allow him to reach full stature and save his country.
notes: Speakers: Socrates and Adeimantus -- 'Not the greatest' = private virtue is inferior to the public good the philosopher could achieve as ruler -- 'Saviour of his country' = the philosopher-king ideal -- This sets up the next question: does any existing state suit philosophy? (The answer will be no -- 6.35) -- The transition from explaining philosophy's bad reputation to the question of implementation -- Cf. Book VII 519c-521b: the philosopher must be compelled to return to the Cave to govern

narrative: "Not any of them," I said; "and that is precisely the accusation which I bring against them -- not one of them is worthy of the philosophic nature, and hence that nature is warped and estranged; -- as the exotic seed which is sown in a foreign land becomes denaturalized, and is wont to be overpowered and to lose itself in the new soil, even so this growth of philosophy, instead of persisting, degenerates and receives another character. But if philosophy ever finds in the State that perfection which she herself is, then will be seen that she is in truth divine, and that all other things, whether natures of men or institutions, are but human; -- and now, I know that you are going to ask, what that State is."
refashioned:
"Not one of them," I said. "And that is precisely my charge against them -- not a single existing government is worthy of the philosophical nature. That's why the philosophical nature gets warped and alienated.

It's like an exotic seed planted in foreign soil: it becomes denaturalized, overwhelmed, and loses its true character. In the same way, the growth of philosophy degenerates and takes on a different character in an unsuitable state.

But if philosophy ever finds a state as perfect as she herself is, then it will become clear that she is truly divine -- and that everything else, whether human natures or institutions, is merely human.

And now -- I know you're about to ask what that state would be."
elegant: No existing government is worthy of the philosophical nature, which is why it becomes warped -- like an exotic seed that degenerates in foreign soil. But if philosophy ever found a state as perfect as herself, she would be revealed as truly divine, and everything else as merely human.
summary: Socrates declares that no existing government is suitable for philosophy, which is why philosophical natures are warped like exotic seeds in foreign soil. Only a state as perfect as philosophy herself would reveal her divine nature.
distilled: No existing state deserves the philosophical nature -- philosophy degenerates in foreign soil, but in the right state, she would be revealed as truly divine.
notes: Speakers: Socrates and Adeimantus -- The exotic seed metaphor: philosophy requires the right political 'soil' to flourish -- 'Denaturalized' = the philosophical nature takes on alien characteristics in a hostile environment -- 'She is in truth divine' = philosophy's nature is divine when uncorrupted -- The radical claim: all existing governments are inadequate for philosophy -- This leads to the question of whether the ideal state they're constructing is that suitable state -- Cf. the plant/seed metaphor from 6.17-18

narrative:
"No," he said; "there you are wrong, for I was going to ask another question -- whether it is the State of which we are the founders and inventors, or some other?"

"Yes," I replied, "ours in most respects; but you may remember my saying before, that some living authority would always be required in the State having the same idea of the constitution which guided you when as legislator you were laying down the laws."

"That was said," he replied.

"Yes, but not in a satisfactory manner; you frightened us by interposing objections, which certainly showed that the discussion would be long and difficult; and what still remains is the reverse of easy."

"What is there remaining?"

"The question how the study of philosophy may be so ordered as not to be the ruin of the State: All great attempts are attended with risk; 'hard is the good,' as men say."

"Still," he said, "let the point be cleared up, and the enquiry will then be complete."

"I shall not be hindered," I said, "by any want of will, but, if at all, by a want of power: my zeal you may see for yourselves; and please to remark in what I am about to say how boldly and unhesitatingly I declare that States should pursue philosophy, not as they do now, but in a different spirit."
refashioned:
"No," he said, "you've guessed wrong. I was going to ask whether it's the state we've been designing, or some other."

"Ours, in most respects," I replied. "But you may recall my saying that the state would always need a living authority who shares the same vision of the constitution that guided you when you were drafting the laws."

"You did say that," he replied.

"Yes, but not in a way that was fully satisfying. Your objections showed that this discussion would be long and difficult -- and what remains is far from easy."

"What remains?"

"The question of how philosophy can be pursued without destroying the state. All great undertakings carry risk. As the saying goes, 'the good is hard.'"

"Still," he said, "let's clear this up and the inquiry will be complete."

"I won't be held back by lack of will," I said, "but perhaps by lack of ability. You can see my enthusiasm. And note how boldly and without hesitation I declare: states should pursue philosophy, but not in the way they currently do -- in an entirely different spirit."
elegant: The ideal state is essentially the one they've been designing, but it needs a living authority who embodies its founding vision. The remaining question is how philosophy can be pursued without ruining the state. Socrates boldly declares that states should pursue philosophy, but in an entirely different spirit from the current approach.
summary: Socrates confirms that the ideal state for philosophy is essentially the one they've been constructing. The remaining challenge is how to integrate philosophy without destroying the state. He boldly declares that states must approach philosophy in a fundamentally different way.
distilled: States must pursue philosophy in a fundamentally different spirit from the current approach -- the key question is how to do so without courting ruin.
notes: Speakers: Socrates and Adeimantus -- 'Some living authority' = the philosopher-ruler who maintains the constitution's guiding vision -- 'Hard is the good' (chalepa ta kala) = a Greek proverb -- Socrates acknowledges the danger: badly implemented philosophy can destroy a state -- The 'different spirit' = the reformed educational program outlined in what follows and in Book VII -- This passage transitions from diagnosing philosophy's problems to proposing solutions

narrative:
"In what manner?"

"At present," I said, "the students of philosophy are quite young; beginning when they are hardly past childhood, they devote only the time saved from moneymaking and housekeeping to such pursuits; and even those of them who are reputed to have most of the philosophic spirit, when they come within sight of the great difficulty of the subject, I mean dialectic, take themselves off. In after life when invited by some one else, they may, perhaps, go and hear a lecture, and about this they make much ado, for philosophy is not considered by them to be their proper business: at last, when they grow old, in most cases they are extinguished more truly than Heracleitus' sun, inasmuch as they never light up again."
refashioned:
"In what way?"

"As things stand now," I said, "philosophy students are very young. They begin barely past childhood and devote only spare time -- whatever's left over from earning money and managing households -- to philosophical studies. Even the ones considered most philosophically gifted, when they get close to the real difficulty -- dialectic -- they quit.

Later in life, if someone invites them to a lecture, they might attend and make a big deal of it. But they don't consider philosophy their real business. And when they grow old, most of them are snuffed out more completely than Heraclitus' sun -- because at least his sun rises again each morning, while they never relight."
elegant: Currently, philosophy is studied only by the young, who abandon it when they encounter dialectic's difficulty. Later they may attend a lecture or two, but never consider philosophy their real vocation. In old age, they are extinguished more permanently than Heraclitus' sun -- for unlike it, they never rise again.
summary: Socrates criticizes the current approach to philosophy: it is studied casually by the young, who abandon it at the first real difficulty. In later life, philosophy is treated as a hobby. By old age, their philosophical light has gone out permanently.
distilled: Philosophy is currently studied casually by the young and abandoned at the first difficulty -- by old age, the philosophical light is permanently extinguished.
notes: Speakers: Socrates and Adeimantus -- 'Heraclitus' sun' = Heraclitus of Ephesus taught that the sun is new every day (Fragment B6), extinguished each night but relit each morning -- The critique targets the Athenian approach to philosophical education -- 'Dialectic' = the highest form of philosophical inquiry, involving rigorous argument about first principles -- The comparison: Heraclitus' sun at least relights; these students never do -- This sets up the contrasting proposal: philosophy should be the culmination of education, not its beginning (6.38) -- Cf. Book VII 537d-539d: the proper age for dialectic is after 30

narrative:
"But what ought to be their course?"

"Just the opposite. In childhood and youth their study, and what philosophy they learn, should be suited to their tender years: during this period while they are growing up towards manhood, the chief and special care should be given to their bodies that they may have them to use in the service of philosophy; as life advances and the intellect begins to mature, let them increase the gymnastics of the soul; but when the strength of our citizens fails and is past civil and military duties, then let them range at will and engage in no serious labour, as we intend them to live happily here, and to crown this life with a similar happiness in another."
refashioned:
"Then what should the proper course be?"

"Exactly the opposite. In childhood and youth, whatever philosophy they study should be suited to their age. During these growing years, the main focus should be on their bodies -- building the physical foundation they'll need in philosophy's service.

As life advances and the intellect matures, increase the exercise of the mind. But when their physical strength declines and they're past the age of civic and military duties -- then let them range freely in philosophy and do nothing else. We want them to live happily in this life and crown it with similar happiness in the next."
elegant: The proper order reverses the current one: in youth, focus on the body while studying age-appropriate philosophy; as the intellect matures, intensify mental training; and when past civic and military duties, devote life entirely to philosophy -- living happily in this world and the next.
summary: Socrates proposes reversing the current educational order: youth should focus on physical development with age-appropriate philosophy, intensifying mental training as the intellect matures, with full philosophical devotion reserved for those past the age of civic and military duties.
distilled: Philosophy should crown a life of physical and civic development, not begin and end in youth -- the current order must be reversed.
notes: Speakers: Socrates and Adeimantus -- This reverses the Athenian practice described in 6.37 -- 'Gymnastics of the soul' = intellectual training as the mental counterpart to physical exercise -- The educational program: body first, then mind, then pure philosophy in maturity -- This anticipates the detailed curriculum of Book VII: arithmetic, geometry, astronomy, harmonics, dialectic -- 'Happiness in another' = the afterlife, connecting to the myth of Er (Book X) and the Phaedo's arguments for immortality -- Cf. Book VII 537d-540a for the specific age-based curriculum

narrative:
"How truly in earnest you are, Socrates!" he said; "I am sure of that; and yet most of your hearers, if I am not mistaken, are likely to be still more earnest in their opposition to you, and will never be convinced; Thrasymachus least of all."

"Do not make a quarrel," I said, "between Thrasymachus and me, who have recently become friends, although, indeed, we were never enemies; for I shall go on striving to the utmost until I either convert him and other men, or do something which may profit them against the day when they live again, and hold the like discourse in another state of existence."
refashioned:
"You're clearly speaking from the heart, Socrates!" he said. "I can see that. But if I'm not mistaken, most of your listeners will oppose you even more passionately and will never be convinced -- Thrasymachus least of all."

"Don't stir up trouble between Thrasymachus and me," I said. "We've recently become friends -- though we were never really enemies. I'll keep striving with everything I have until I either convert him and others, or at least do something that benefits them when they live again and have similar conversations in another life."
elegant: Adeimantus warns that the audience -- especially Thrasymachus -- will fiercely resist. Socrates responds that he and Thrasymachus are friends now, and he will keep striving to convert them in this life or benefit them in the next.
summary: Adeimantus warns that most listeners, especially Thrasymachus, will resist these ideas. Socrates responds warmly that he and Thrasymachus are friends, and he will persist in trying to convert them -- in this life or the next.
distilled: Socrates will keep striving to convert even his fiercest opponents, whether in this life or in future existences.
notes: Speakers: Socrates and Adeimantus -- Thrasymachus = the aggressive interlocutor from Book I who argued that justice is the advantage of the stronger -- 'Recently become friends' = Thrasymachus was tamed by Socratic argument in Book I -- 'Another state of existence' = a reference to the transmigration of souls (metempsychosis), a Pythagorean and Orphic doctrine adopted by Plato -- Cf. Phaedo 70c-72e on the cycle of lives -- This is a lighter, more personal moment in the dialogue -- Socrates' patience and goodwill toward opponents is characteristic

narrative:
"You are speaking of a time which is not very near."

"Rather," I replied, "of a time which is as nothing in comparison with eternity. Nevertheless, I do not wonder that the many refuse to believe; for they have never seen that of which we are now speaking realised; they have seen only a conventional imitation of philosophy, consisting of words artificially brought together, not like these of ours having a natural unity. But a human being who in word and work is perfectly moulded, as far as he can be, into the proportion and likeness of virtue -- such a man ruling in a city which bears the same image, they have never yet seen, neither one nor many of them -- do you think that they ever did?"
refashioned:
"You're talking about something that's not exactly around the corner."

"Perhaps," I replied, "but it's nothing compared to eternity. Still, I'm not surprised the public refuses to believe any of this. They've never seen what we're describing actually realized. All they've seen is a conventional imitation of philosophy -- words artificially stitched together, unlike our discussion which has a natural unity.

A human being perfectly molded, in both word and deed, into the form and likeness of virtue -- and such a person ruling a city that bears the same image -- they've never seen this, not even once. Have they?"
elegant: The public refuses to believe because they have never seen genuine philosophy realized -- only conventional imitations made of artificially assembled words. They have never witnessed a person perfectly shaped into the likeness of virtue ruling a city molded in the same image.
summary: Socrates explains public disbelief: people have never seen genuine philosophy realized, only artificial imitations. They have never witnessed a truly virtuous person ruling a city shaped in virtue's image.
distilled: The public disbelieves in philosopher-kings because they have never seen either genuine philosophy or a truly virtuous ruler -- only artificial imitations.
notes: Speakers: Socrates and Adeimantus -- 'As nothing in comparison with eternity' = the philosophical perspective on time, cf. the 'spectator of all time' (6.4) -- 'Conventional imitation of philosophy' = the sophistic or rhetorical version of philosophy known in Athens -- 'Words artificially brought together' vs. 'natural unity' = the difference between sophistic rhetoric and genuine dialectic -- 'Perfectly moulded into the proportion and likeness of virtue' = the philosopher-king as a living embodiment of the Forms -- The city 'bearing the same image' = the just state as an image of justice itself -- Cf. Book VII: the philosopher who returns from seeing the Good to govern

narrative:
"No, my friend, and they have seldom, if ever, heard free and noble sentiments; such as men utter when they are earnestly and by every means in their power seeking after truth for the sake of knowledge, while they look coldly on the subtleties of controversy, of which the end is opinion and strife, whether they meet with them in the courts of law or in society."

"They are strangers," he said, "to the words of which you speak."
refashioned:
"No, my friend. And they have rarely, if ever, heard free and noble speech -- the kind that comes from people who are earnestly and by every means pursuing truth for the sake of knowledge itself, while looking with contempt on the clever tricks of controversy whose only products are opinion and conflict, whether in the courtroom or in social life."

"They are complete strangers," he said, "to the kind of discourse you're describing."
elegant: The public has rarely heard free, noble speech -- the kind that comes from genuinely pursuing truth for its own sake, as opposed to the clever controversies of courtrooms and social life that produce only opinion and strife.
summary: Socrates observes that the public has rarely heard genuine philosophical discourse -- speech motivated by the pure pursuit of truth, as opposed to the competitive debates of courtrooms and social gatherings that aim only at opinion and victory.
distilled: The public has never encountered speech that earnestly seeks truth for its own sake -- they know only the competitive controversies of courts and society.
notes: Speakers: Socrates and Adeimantus -- 'Free and noble sentiments' = parrhesia (frank speech) in the service of truth -- Contrast between philosophical inquiry (seeking truth) and eristic argument (seeking victory) -- 'Courts of law' = Athenian courts where rhetorical skill determined outcomes -- 'Opinion and strife' = doxa and eris, the products of sophistic debate -- Cf. Gorgias 454d-455a: the distinction between persuasion that produces knowledge and persuasion that produces belief -- The passage implicitly criticizes Athenian democratic culture

narrative: "And this was what we foresaw, and this was the reason why truth forced us to admit, not without fear and hesitation, that neither cities nor States nor individuals will ever attain perfection until the small class of philosophers whom we termed useless but not corrupt are providentially compelled, whether they will or not, to take care of the State, and until a like necessity be laid on the State to obey them; or until kings, or if not kings, the sons of kings or princes, are divinely inspired with a true love of true philosophy. That either or both of these alternatives are impossible, I see no reason to affirm: if they were so, we might indeed be justly ridiculed as dreamers and visionaries. Am I not right?"
refashioned:
"This is exactly what we anticipated, and it's why truth compelled us -- not without fear and hesitation -- to make this admission: neither cities nor individuals will ever achieve perfection until the small class of philosophers we called useless but not corrupt are somehow compelled, whether they want to or not, to take charge of the state -- and the state is equally compelled to obey them. Or alternatively, until kings, or the sons of kings, are divinely inspired with a genuine love of genuine philosophy.

I see no reason to declare either of these alternatives impossible. If they were, we could rightly be mocked as dreamers and visionaries. Am I wrong?"
elegant: Neither cities nor individuals will attain perfection until either uncorrupt philosophers are compelled to rule and the state to obey them, or kings become genuinely inspired by philosophy. Neither alternative is impossible -- if they were, the entire argument would be mere dreaming.
summary: Socrates restates the central thesis: perfection requires either compelling philosophers to rule (and the state to obey), or inspiring kings with genuine love of philosophy. He insists neither alternative is impossible.
distilled: Cities will never achieve perfection until philosophers are compelled to rule or kings are inspired with genuine philosophy -- and neither alternative is impossible.
notes: Speakers: Socrates and Adeimantus -- This restates the philosopher-king thesis from Book V (473c-d) -- 'Useless but not corrupt' = the distinction established earlier between good but withdrawn philosophers and bad pretenders -- 'Providentially compelled' = divine necessity (theia anangke) -- Two paths to the ideal state: (1) philosophers compelled to rule, (2) rulers becoming philosophers -- 'Sons of kings' = an acknowledgment that transformation might happen generationally -- 'Dreamers and visionaries' = Socrates acknowledges the charge but argues the dream is achievable -- Cf. Book VII 520a-521b: the philosopher must be compelled to return to the Cave

narrative:
"Quite right."

"If then, in the countless ages of the past, or at the present hour in some foreign clime which is far away and beyond our ken, the perfected philosopher is or has been or hereafter shall be compelled by a superior power to have the charge of the State, we are ready to assert to the death, that this our constitution has been, and is -- yea, and will be whenever the Muse of Philosophy is queen. There is no impossibility in all this; that there is a difficulty, we acknowledge ourselves."

"My opinion agrees with yours," he said.

"But do you mean to say that this is not the opinion of the multitude?"
refashioned:
"Exactly right."

"If, in the countless ages past, or right now in some distant land beyond our knowledge, or at any time in the future, a perfected philosopher has been, is, or will be compelled by a higher power to take charge of the state -- then we are ready to assert to the death that our constitution has existed, does exist, and will exist whenever the Muse of Philosophy reigns supreme. There is nothing impossible in any of this. That it's difficult, we freely admit."

"I agree with you," he said.

"But would you say this is also the opinion of the general public?"
elegant: Whether in the distant past, in some foreign land, or in the future, whenever a perfected philosopher is compelled to govern, the ideal constitution becomes real. This is not impossible, merely difficult. The Muse of Philosophy can reign -- the question is whether the public will ever believe it.
summary: Socrates declares with conviction that whenever and wherever a perfected philosopher is compelled to govern, their ideal constitution becomes reality. He insists this is difficult but not impossible, and asks whether the public could ever accept this view.
distilled: The ideal constitution is realized whenever a perfected philosopher governs -- this is not impossible but difficult, and the public remains skeptical.
notes: Speakers: Socrates and Adeimantus -- 'Assert to the death' = Socrates' passionate commitment to the philosopher-king ideal -- 'Muse of Philosophy' = philosophy personified as divine inspiration -- 'Some foreign clime' = acknowledges that the ideal state may exist outside Greece -- 'Countless ages of the past' = the ideal may already have existed historically -- The cosmopolitan scope is notable: the ideal is not limited to Greek civilization -- 'Difficulty' vs. 'impossibility' = a crucial distinction Socrates maintains throughout

narrative:
"I should imagine not," he replied.

"O my friend," I said, "do not attack the multitude: they will change their minds, if, not in an aggressive spirit, but gently and with the view of soothing them and removing their dislike of over-education, you show them your philosophers as they really are and describe as you were just now doing their character and profession, and then mankind will see that he of whom you are speaking is not such as they supposed -- if they view him in this new light, they will surely change their notion of him, and answer in another strain. Who can be at enmity with one who loves them, who that is himself gentle and free from envy will be jealous of one in whom there is no jealousy? Nay, let me answer for you, that in a few this harsh temper may be found but not in the majority of mankind."
refashioned:
"I'd imagine not," he replied.

"My friend," I said, "don't attack the multitude. They will change their minds -- if, instead of being aggressive, you approach them gently, aiming to soothe them and remove their suspicion of excessive intellectualism. Show them what philosophers are really like. Describe their character and vocation as you just did.

Then people will see that the philosopher isn't what they imagined. Viewing him in this new light, they'll surely revise their opinion and speak differently. Who can be hostile to someone who loves them? Who, being gentle and free from envy himself, would resent someone equally free of jealousy?

Let me answer for you: this harsh attitude may be found in a few people, but not in the majority of humankind."
elegant: Don't attack the multitude -- approach them gently, showing them what philosophers truly are. Once they see the philosopher as he really is -- gentle, loving, free from envy -- they will change their minds. Such hostility exists in a few, but not in the majority.
summary: Socrates counsels against attacking the public. Instead, by gently showing them the true nature of philosophers -- gentle, loving, free from envy -- the public can be won over. Most people's hostility toward philosophy is based on misunderstanding, not inherent malice.
distilled: The public can be persuaded to accept philosophers if approached gently and shown what genuine philosophy truly looks like -- most hostility stems from misunderstanding.
notes: Speakers: Socrates and Adeimantus -- A surprisingly optimistic passage about public persuasion -- 'Dislike of over-education' = the Athenian suspicion of excessive intellectualism -- Socrates advocates gentle persuasion rather than confrontation -- 'Free from envy' (aphthonos) = a key philosophical virtue -- The argument: the public's hostility is based on false impressions, not inherent opposition -- This represents Socrates' hope that the philosopher-king ideal is practically achievable -- Contrasts with his more pessimistic moments (6.21, 6.33)

narrative:
"I quite agree with you," he said.

"And do you not also think, as I do, that the harsh feeling which the many entertain towards philosophy originates in the pretenders, who rush in uninvited, and are always abusing them, and finding fault with them, who make persons instead of things the theme of their conversation? and nothing can be more unbecoming in philosophers than this."
refashioned:
"I completely agree," he said.

"And don't you think, as I do, that the public's hostility toward philosophy originates with the pretenders -- people who barge in uninvited, constantly criticizing and finding fault, making personalities rather than ideas the subject of their conversation? Nothing could be more unbecoming for a philosopher than this."
elegant: The public's hostility toward philosophy originates with pretenders who rush in uninvited, constantly criticizing people instead of discussing ideas. Nothing is more unbecoming in a philosopher than making personalities the theme of conversation.
summary: Socrates argues that public hostility toward philosophy comes from pretenders who make personal attacks rather than discussing ideas. Making personalities the subject of conversation is the most unphilosophical thing a philosopher can do.
distilled: Public hostility toward philosophy originates with pretenders who attack people rather than discuss ideas -- the most unphilosophical behavior possible.
notes: Speakers: Socrates and Adeimantus -- 'Pretenders who rush in uninvited' = the false philosophers described in 6.31-32 -- 'Persons instead of things' = ad hominem attacks vs. philosophical inquiry into truth -- This distinction between discussing people and discussing ideas is fundamental to Socratic method -- Cf. the Sophists who were known for personal attacks and display speeches -- Socrates practiced what he preached: his method targeted ideas and definitions, not personalities (despite the Aristophanic caricature)

narrative:
"It is most unbecoming."

"For he, Adeimantus, whose mind is fixed upon true being, has surely no time to look down upon the affairs of earth, or to be filled with malice and envy, contending against men; his eye is ever directed towards things fixed and immutable, which he sees neither injuring nor injured by one another, but all in order moving according to reason; these he imitates, and to these he will, as far as he can, conform himself. Can a man help imitating that with which he holds reverential converse?"
refashioned:
"It is completely unbecoming."

"For the person whose mind is fixed on true being, Adeimantus, has no time to look down on earthly affairs or fill himself with malice and envy, quarreling with other people. His gaze is always directed toward things that are fixed and immutable -- things he sees neither harming nor being harmed by one another, all moving in order according to reason.

These are what he imitates. These are what he tries, as far as he can, to make himself resemble. And can anyone help imitating that with which he holds reverential communion?"
elegant: The philosopher whose mind is fixed on true being has no time for earthly quarrels, malice, or envy. His gaze is always on the fixed and immutable -- things that neither harm nor are harmed, all moving in rational order. He inevitably imitates what he contemplates with reverence.
summary: Socrates explains that the true philosopher, fixed on contemplating eternal being, has no time for earthly malice or envy. He gazes on the immutable and rational, and inevitably comes to imitate and resemble what he contemplates.
distilled: The philosopher inevitably becomes like what he contemplates -- gazing on the fixed and rational, he has no time for earthly malice or envy.
notes: Speakers: Socrates and Adeimantus -- 'Things fixed and immutable' = the Forms -- 'Neither injuring nor injured' = the Forms exist in perfect harmony, unlike the sensible world -- 'Moving according to reason' = the rational order of the intelligible world -- The principle of assimilation: you become like what you contemplate (homoiosis theo) -- Cf. Theaetetus 176b: 'becoming like God as far as possible' -- This principle is central to Plato's ethics: contemplation of the Good transforms the contemplator -- Anticipates the philosopher-as-artist metaphor: the philosopher copies the divine pattern

narrative:
"Impossible."

"And the philosopher holding converse with the divine order, becomes orderly and divine, as far as the nature of man allows; but like every one else, he will suffer from detraction."

"Of course."

"And if a necessity be laid upon him of fashioning, not only himself, but human nature generally, whether in States or individuals, into that which he beholds elsewhere, will he, think you, be an unskilful artificer of justice, temperance, and every civil virtue?"

"Anything but unskilful."

"And if the world perceives that what we are saying about him is the truth, will they be angry with philosophy? Will they disbelieve us, when we tell them that no State can be happy which is not designed by artists who imitate the heavenly pattern?"

"They will not be angry if they understand," he said.

"But how will they draw out the plan of which you are speaking?"

"They will begin by taking the State and the manners of men, from which, as from a tablet, they will rub out the picture, and leave a clean surface. This is no easy task. But whether easy or not, herein will lie the difference between them and every other legislator, -- they will have nothing to do either with individual or State, and will inscribe no laws, until they have either found, or themselves made, a clean surface."

"They will be very right," he said.

"Having effected this, they will proceed to trace an outline of the constitution?"
refashioned:
"Impossible not to."

"And the philosopher, holding communion with the divine order, becomes orderly and divine himself -- as far as human nature allows. Though like everyone else, he'll face criticism."

"Of course."

"And if he's called upon to shape not only himself but human nature generally -- in individuals and in states -- into the likeness of what he beholds in the higher realm, will he be a poor craftsman of justice, temperance, and every civic virtue?"

"Far from it."

"And if the world comes to see that what we're saying is true, will they be angry at philosophy? Will they refuse to believe that no state can be happy unless it's designed by artists who imitate the heavenly pattern?"

"They won't be angry if they understand," he said.

"But how will these philosopher-artists create their design?"

"They'll start by taking the state and the characters of its people -- as if these were a canvas -- and wiping it clean. This is no easy task. But easy or not, here is what distinguishes them from every other legislator: they will not touch either individual or state, they will inscribe no laws, until they have either found or created a clean surface."

"That's absolutely right," he said.

"Once they've accomplished this, they'll begin sketching the outline of the constitution?"
elegant: The philosopher, communing with the divine order, becomes orderly and divine himself. Called upon to shape human nature into the likeness of what he contemplates, he becomes a skilled craftsman of justice and virtue. Like an artist, the philosopher-legislator wipes the canvas clean before sketching the constitution -- he inscribes no laws until the surface is prepared. No state can be happy unless designed by artists who imitate the heavenly pattern.
summary: Socrates describes the philosopher as both contemplative and creative: through communion with the divine order, he becomes divine himself, then acts as a skilled artist-legislator who wipes the political canvas clean before designing a constitution modeled on the heavenly pattern.
distilled: The philosopher becomes divine through contemplation, then acts as an artist-legislator who wipes the canvas clean and designs the state after the heavenly pattern.
notes: Speakers: Socrates and Adeimantus -- The philosopher-as-artist (demiourgos) metaphor is central here -- 'Heavenly pattern' (paradeigma) = the Forms, especially the Form of the Good -- 'Wiping the canvas clean' = radical political reform, starting from scratch -- This anticipates modern revolutionary political theory -- 'No state can be happy unless designed by artists who imitate the heavenly pattern' = the philosopher-king thesis in aesthetic terms -- Cf. Timaeus: the Demiurge crafts the cosmos by looking to the eternal Forms -- The 'clean surface' requirement explains why philosopher-kings need total political authority -- Cf. Book VII 540d-541a: the philosophers must send away everyone over ten and start fresh

narrative:
"No doubt."

"And when they are filling in the work, as I conceive, they will often turn their eyes upwards and downwards: I mean that they will first look at absolute justice and beauty and temperance, and again at the human copy; and will mingle and temper the various elements of life into the image of a man; and thus they will conceive according to that other image, which, when existing among men, Homer calls the form and likeness of God."
refashioned:
"No doubt."

"And as they fill in the details, I imagine they will look constantly back and forth -- upward to absolute justice, beauty, and temperance, then downward to the human copy they're creating. They'll blend and adjust the various elements of life to produce the image of a truly good person. Their model will be that higher image which, when it appears among human beings, Homer calls 'the form and likeness of God.'"
elegant: The philosopher-artists look constantly between the Forms above -- absolute justice, beauty, temperance -- and the human copy below, blending the elements of life to create a person in 'the form and likeness of God,' as Homer says.
summary: As philosopher-legislators design the constitution, they look constantly between the eternal Forms of justice, beauty, and temperance and their human copy, blending life's elements to create what Homer calls 'the form and likeness of God.'
distilled: The philosopher-legislator looks back and forth between absolute virtue and human reality, crafting citizens in the form and likeness of God.
notes: Speakers: Socrates and Adeimantus -- 'Turn their eyes upwards and downwards' = the philosopher-artist's gaze alternating between the Forms and the material -- The painter metaphor: the Forms are the model, the state and its citizens are the canvas -- 'Form and likeness of God' = Homer, Odyssey or Iliad (various passages describe godlike heroes) -- The philosopher creates humans who embody divine qualities -- This connects to the homoiosis theo ('becoming like God') theme from 6.46 -- The 'mingling and tempering' suggests that the ideal is achieved through careful adjustment, not rigid imposition -- Cf. Timaeus 29a-30c: the Demiurge mixes elements while looking to eternal paradigms

narrative:
"Very true," he said.

"And one feature they will erase, and another they will put in, until they have made the ways of men, as far as possible, agreeable to the ways of God?"

"Indeed," he said, "in no way could they make a fairer picture."

"And now," I said, "are we beginning to persuade those whom you described as rushing at us with might and main, that the painter of constitutions is such an one as we are praising; at whom they were so very indignant because to his hands we committed the State; and are they growing a little calmer at what they have just heard?"

"Much calmer, if there is any sense in them."

"Why, where can they still find any ground for objection? Will they doubt that the philosopher is a lover of truth and being?"

"They would not be so unreasonable."

"Or that his nature, being such as we have delineated, is akin to the highest good?"

"Neither can they doubt this."

"But again, will they tell us that such a nature, placed under favourable circumstances, will not be perfectly good and wise if any ever was? Or will they prefer those whom we have rejected?"

"Surely not."

"Then will they still be angry at our saying, that, until philosophers bear rule, States and individuals will have no rest from evil, nor will this our imaginary State ever be realised?"

"I think that they will be less angry."

"Shall we assume that they are not only less angry but quite gentle, and that they have been converted and for very shame, if for no other reason, cannot refuse to come to terms?"

"By all means," he said.

"Then let us suppose that the reconciliation has been effected. Will any one deny the other point, that there may be sons of kings or princes who are by nature philosophers?"

"Surely no man," he said.

"And when they have come into being will any one say that they must of necessity be destroyed; that they can hardly be saved is not denied even by us; but that in the whole course of ages no single one of them can escape -- who will venture to affirm this?"
refashioned:
"Very true," he said.

"They'll erase one feature and add another until they've made human ways as close as possible to divine ways?"

"Indeed," he said. "They couldn't possibly paint a finer picture."

"Now," I said, "are we beginning to persuade those critics you described as charging at us? The ones who were so outraged that we entrusted the state to a 'painter of constitutions' -- are they calming down?"

"Much calmer, if they have any sense."

"Where can they still object? Will they deny that the philosopher loves truth and being?"

"They wouldn't be that unreasonable."

"Or that his nature, as we've described it, is akin to the highest good?"

"They can't deny that either."

"Will they claim that such a nature, given favorable conditions, won't become perfectly good and wise? Or would they prefer the people we've already rejected?"

"Surely not."

"Then will they still be angry when we say that until philosophers rule, states and individuals will find no rest from evil, and our ideal state will never be realized?"

"I think they'll be less angry."

"Can we assume they're not just less angry but actually persuaded -- converted, even if only out of embarrassment, and unable to refuse our terms?"

"By all means," he said.

"Then suppose the reconciliation is complete. Will anyone deny that sons of kings or princes might be natural philosophers?"

"No one would deny that."

"And once such philosopher-princes are born, will anyone claim they must inevitably be corrupted? Even we admit they'll struggle to survive. But will anyone venture to say that across all the ages, not a single one can ever escape corruption?"
elegant: The philosopher-artist erases and adds until human ways approximate divine ways -- the fairest possible picture. No reasonable person can deny that the philosopher loves truth, is akin to the highest good, and would govern wisely under favorable conditions. Sons of kings may be born philosophers, and across all ages, it is inconceivable that not a single one can survive corruption to rule wisely.
summary: Socrates argues that the case for philosopher-kings is now overwhelming: the philosopher's nature is undeniably akin to the highest good. Even if most philosopher-princes are corrupted, across all ages it is impossible that not a single one survives to realize the ideal state.
distilled: Given infinite time, at least one philosopher-prince must survive corruption -- making the ideal state difficult but not impossible.
notes: Speakers: Socrates and Adeimantus -- 'Painter of constitutions' = the philosopher-as-artist metaphor continued -- The argument from probability: across infinite time, even unlikely events must occur -- 'Sons of kings' = the second path to the ideal state: rulers who become philosophers -- This is Socrates' most sustained effort to show the ideal is practically achievable -- 'For very shame' = an interesting psychological argument: even critics should feel embarrassed to deny the logic -- The series of rhetorical questions builds an irresistible case -- Cf. Book V 473c-d: the original statement of the philosopher-king thesis

narrative:
"Who indeed!"

"But," said I, "one is enough; let there be one man who has a city obedient to his will, and he might bring into existence the ideal polity about which the world is so incredulous."

"Yes, one is enough."

"The ruler may impose the laws and institutions which we have been describing, and the citizens may possibly be willing to obey them?"

"Certainly."

"And that others should approve of what we approve, is no miracle or impossibility?"

"I think not."

"But we have sufficiently shown, in what has preceded, that all this, if only possible, is assuredly for the best."

"We have."

"And now we say not only that our laws, if they could be enacted, would be for the best, but also that the enactment of them, though difficult, is not impossible."

"Very good."

"And so with pain and toil we have reached the end of one subject, but more remains to be discussed; -- how and by what studies and pursuits will the saviours of the constitution be created, and at what ages are they to apply themselves to their several studies?"
refashioned:
"Who indeed!"

"And one is enough," I said. "Let there be just one person with a city willing to follow his lead, and he could bring the ideal state into existence -- the one the world considers impossible."

"Yes, one is enough."

"The ruler could establish the laws and institutions we've described, and the citizens might well be willing to obey?"

"Certainly."

"And there's nothing miraculous or impossible about others approving what we approve?"

"I think not."

"We've already shown that these arrangements, if achievable, would be the best possible."

"We have."

"So now we're saying not only that our laws would be best if enacted, but also that enacting them, while difficult, is not impossible."

"Very good."

"With great effort, we've reached the end of one subject. But more remains: by what studies and pursuits will the saviors of the constitution be trained, and at what ages should they take up each study?"
elegant: One philosopher-ruler is enough -- just one person with a willing city could realize the ideal state. The laws described are the best possible, and their enactment, while difficult, is not impossible. The remaining question: what education will produce these saviors of the constitution, and at what ages?
summary: Socrates declares that just one philosopher with an obedient city could bring the ideal state into existence. Having established that these laws are best and their enactment possible, the discussion turns to the education that will produce the constitution's saviors.
distilled: One philosopher-ruler with a willing city is enough to realize the ideal state -- the remaining question is what education will produce such rulers.
notes: Speakers: Socrates and Adeimantus -- 'One is enough' = a strikingly practical claim that tempers the idealism -- This transitions from defending the philosopher-king concept to designing the educational curriculum -- 'Saviours of the constitution' = the philosopher-rulers as preservers of the ideal state -- 'At what ages' anticipates the age-based curriculum of Book VII: mathematics in youth, dialectic after 30, practical politics after 35, pure philosophy after 50 -- 'Pain and toil' = acknowledging the difficulty of the argument so far -- The word 'studies' (mathemata) will lead directly into the discussion of the mathematical curriculum

narrative:
"Certainly."

"I omitted the troublesome business of the possession of women, and the procreation of children, and the appointment of the rulers, because I knew that the perfect State would be eyed with jealousy and was difficult of attainment; but that piece of cleverness was not of much service to me, for I had to discuss them all the same. The women and children are now disposed of, but the other question of the rulers must be investigated from the very beginning. We were saying, as you will remember, that they were to be lovers of their country, tried by the test of pleasures and pains, and neither in hardships, nor in dangers, nor at any other critical moment were to lose their patriotism -- he was to be rejected who failed, but he who always came forth pure, like gold tried in the refiner's fire, was to be made a ruler, and to receive honours and rewards in life and after death. This was the sort of thing which was being said, and then the argument turned aside and veiled her face; not liking to stir the question which has now arisen."
refashioned:
"Certainly."

"I deliberately skipped the difficult questions about women's roles, child-rearing, and the appointment of rulers, knowing the ideal state would provoke jealousy and would be hard to achieve. But my evasion didn't help much -- I had to deal with them anyway. The questions about women and children have now been settled, but the question of rulers must be examined from the start.

You'll recall we said rulers must be lovers of their country, tested through pleasures and pains. In hardship, danger, or any crisis, they must never lose their devotion. Anyone who failed was to be rejected. But whoever emerged pure every time -- like gold tested in a refiner's fire -- was to be made a ruler and receive honors in life and after death.

That's what we were saying, and then the argument turned aside and covered her face -- reluctant to stir up the question that has now come before us."
elegant: The troublesome questions of women, children, and rulers were previously deferred but now demand attention. Rulers must be lovers of their country, tested through pleasure, pain, and danger. Those who emerge pure like gold from fire become rulers. The argument now faces what it previously avoided: who exactly these rulers are.
summary: Socrates acknowledges he previously avoided difficult topics about women, children, and rulers, but must now address them. He recalls that rulers must be tested through pleasures and pains, with those who emerge pure like refined gold appointed to rule. The crucial question of the rulers' identity must now be confronted.
distilled: The rulers must be those who pass every test of pleasure, pain, and danger, emerging pure like gold from fire -- the question of their identity can no longer be deferred.
notes: Speakers: Socrates and Adeimantus -- 'Possession of women' and 'procreation of children' = the controversial proposals of Book V (community of women and children among guardians) -- 'Gold tried in the refiner's fire' = a powerful metaphor for testing character, also used in the 'Allegory of Metals' from Book III -- 'The argument turned aside and veiled her face' = the argument personified as a modest woman -- The test of pleasures and pains was described in Book III (413d-414a) -- This passage marks the transition to the central revelation: the perfect guardian must be a philosopher (6.52)

narrative:
"I perfectly remember," he said.

"Yes, my friend," I said, "and I then shrank from hazarding the bold word; but now let me dare to say -- that the perfect guardian must be a philosopher."

"Yes," he said, "let that be affirmed."

"And do not suppose that there will be many of them; for the gifts which were deemed by us to be essential rarely grow together; they are mostly found in shreds and patches."

"What do you mean?" he said.

"You are aware," I replied, "that quick intelligence, memory, sagacity, cleverness, and similar qualities, do not often grow together, and that persons who possess them and are at the same time high-spirited and magnanimous are not so constituted by nature as to live orderly and in a peaceful and settled manner; they are driven any way by their impulses, and all solid principle goes out of them."

"Very true," he said.

"On the other hand, those steadfast natures which can better be depended upon, which in a battle are impregnable to fear and immovable, are equally immovable when there is anything to be learned; they are always in a torpid state, and are apt to yawn and go to sleep over any intellectual toil."

"Quite true."

"And yet we were saying that both qualities were necessary in those to whom the higher education is to be imparted, and who are to share in any office or command."
refashioned:
"I remember perfectly," he said.

"Yes, my friend," I said. "Back then I shrank from the bold statement. But now let me dare to say it: the perfect guardian must be a philosopher."

"Yes," he said, "let that be affirmed."

"And don't imagine there will be many of them. The gifts we've identified as essential rarely exist together in one person -- they're usually scattered in bits and pieces."

"What do you mean?"

"Think about it," I replied. "Quick intelligence, strong memory, perceptiveness, cleverness -- these qualities don't often combine with high-spiritedness and magnanimity. People who have the intellectual gifts tend to be driven by impulse, living erratically, with no solid principles to anchor them."

"Very true," he said.

"On the other hand, the steady, dependable types -- the ones who are fearless and immovable in battle -- are equally immovable when it comes to learning. They're in a permanent torpor, yawning and falling asleep over any intellectual challenge."

"Exactly."

"Yet we said that both sets of qualities -- intellectual brilliance and steady character -- are necessary for anyone who is to receive the higher education and hold positions of authority."
elegant: The perfect guardian must be a philosopher -- but such people will be few. The essential qualities rarely combine: quick intelligence, memory, and cleverness don't naturally coexist with steadiness and magnanimity. The brilliant are often impulsive and unstable; the steady are often intellectually torpid. Yet both qualities are required for the higher education and positions of authority.
summary: Socrates finally makes the bold declaration: the perfect guardian must be a philosopher. But such people will be rare, because intellectual brilliance and steady character almost never coexist in one person -- the brilliant tend to be unstable, and the steady tend to be intellectually torpid.
distilled: The perfect guardian must be a philosopher, but such natures are exceedingly rare because intellectual brilliance and steady character almost never combine.
notes: Speakers: Socrates and Adeimantus -- 'The bold word' = the philosopher-king thesis, now stated directly for the guardians -- 'Shreds and patches' = the philosopher's virtues scattered across different personality types -- The tension between intellectual and spirited natures maps onto the tripartite soul from Book IV -- Quick/clever but impulsive = rational element unmoored from the spirited -- Steady but torpid = spirited element without rational drive -- The rare combination = the philosopher-king who unites both -- Cf. Book VII: the curriculum is designed precisely to develop both capacities

narrative:
"Certainly," he said.

"And will they be a class which is rarely found?"

"Yes, indeed."

"Then the aspirant must not only be tested in those labours and dangers and pleasures which we mentioned before, but there is another kind of probation which we did not mention -- he must be exercised also in many kinds of knowledge, to see whether the soul will be able to endure the highest of all, or will faint under them, as in any other studies and exercises."
refashioned:
"Certainly," he said.

"And will such people be rare?"

"Extremely rare."

"Then the candidate must be tested not only in the labors, dangers, and pleasures we mentioned before, but also in another way we haven't discussed: he must be put through many kinds of knowledge to see whether his soul can endure the highest studies, or whether he'll collapse under their weight -- just as some people fail in physical training."
elegant: The rare candidates who combine both qualities must be tested not only through labor, danger, and pleasure, but also through many kinds of knowledge -- to determine whether the soul can endure the highest studies or will collapse under them.
summary: Beyond tests of character through labor, danger, and pleasure, the rare philosophical candidates must also be tested through rigorous intellectual training to determine if their souls can withstand the demands of the highest knowledge.
distilled: The philosophical candidate must be tested through rigorous intellectual training to see if his soul can endure the highest knowledge or will collapse under its demands.
notes: Speakers: Socrates and Adeimantus -- The 'highest of all' knowledge = the Form of the Good, about to be introduced -- Previous tests (labors, dangers, pleasures) were from Book III (413d-414a) -- The new test is intellectual: can the soul handle the highest abstractions? -- 'Faint under them' = the intellectual version of physical collapse -- This sets up the question 'what is the highest knowledge?' which leads directly to the Form of the Good -- Cf. Book VII 537d: not everyone can handle dialectic

narrative:
"Yes," he said, "you are quite right in testing him. But what do you mean by the highest of all knowledge?"

"You may remember," I said, "that we divided the soul into three parts; and distinguished the several natures of justice, temperance, courage, and wisdom?"

"Indeed," he said, "if I had forgotten, I should not deserve to hear more."

"And do you remember the word of caution which preceded the discussion of them?"
refashioned:
"Yes," he said, "you're right to test them this way. But what do you mean by the 'highest knowledge'?"

"You may recall," I said, "that we divided the soul into three parts and defined the distinct natures of justice, temperance, courage, and wisdom."

"Indeed," he said, "if I'd forgotten that, I wouldn't deserve to hear any more."

"And do you remember the warning that came before that discussion?"
elegant: What is the highest knowledge? Socrates recalls that they divided the soul into three parts and defined the four cardinal virtues. But a warning preceded that discussion -- one that is now about to become relevant.
summary: When asked what the 'highest knowledge' is, Socrates recalls the tripartite division of the soul and the definitions of the four virtues from Book IV, along with a caveat that preceded that discussion -- one that now becomes critically important.
distilled: The question of the highest knowledge leads back to the soul's division and the virtues, but a crucial earlier caveat is about to be revisited.
notes: Speakers: Socrates and Adeimantus -- 'Three parts of the soul' = rational, spirited, appetitive (Book IV, 435-441) -- 'Justice, temperance, courage, and wisdom' = the four cardinal virtues defined in Book IV -- 'Word of caution' = Socrates' warning in Book IV (435c-d) that they were using a 'shorter way' and not the most precise method -- This sets up the revelation that the Form of the Good is the highest knowledge, surpassing even the virtues themselves -- Adeimantus' reply shows the importance of what came before

narrative:
"To what do you refer?"

"We were saying, if I am not mistaken, that he who wanted to see them in their perfect beauty must take a longer and more circuitous way, at the end of which they would appear; but that we could add on a popular exposition of them on a level with the discussion which had preceded. And you replied that such an exposition would be enough for you, and so the enquiry was continued in what to me seemed to be a very inaccurate manner; whether you were satisfied or not, it is for you to say."
refashioned:
"What are you referring to?"

"We said, if I remember correctly, that anyone who wanted to see the virtues in their perfect beauty would have to take a longer and more circuitous route, at the end of which they would finally appear. But we settled for a popular-level treatment that matched the discussion we'd already been having. You said that would be good enough for you. So we continued in what seemed to me a rather imprecise way -- whether it satisfied you is for you to say."
